‚Źļ Fixed and pushed (8c63091).

  What was wrong

  The demo launches two external MCP servers (mcp-google-gmail,
  mcp-youtube-transcript) that you hadn't installed. The old code looked only
  for a venv console script, so it printed two cryptic No such file or directory
  warnings and then ran a tool-less agent anyway (useless).

  I confirmed the package/console-script names were actually correct ‚ÄĒ they just
  weren't installed (and uv/uvx isn't on your machine either).

  The fix (both before.py and after.py)

  - Robust command resolution: prefer an installed console script (this venv,
  then PATH), falling back to uvx <pkg> when uv is available.
  - Preflight before doing any work: if a server can't be resolved, it now
  prints exactly what to install and exits cleanly instead of running without
  tools.

  Now running it prints:
  This demo needs external MCP servers that aren't installed: mcp-google-gmail,
  mcp-youtube-transcript

  Install them into this environment, then re-run:
      pip install mcp-google-gmail mcp-youtube-transcript
      mcp-google-gmail auth        # one-time Gmail OAuth (opens a browser)

  (Or install uv ‚ÄĒ https://astral.sh/uv ‚ÄĒ and they'll run via uvx
  automatically.)
  Full setup: examples/mcp/gmail_youtube_demo/README.md

  To actually run it

  pip install mcp-google-gmail mcp-youtube-transcript
  mcp-google-gmail auth          # one-time Google OAuth in the browser
  export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=...   # (or OPENROUTER_API_KEY)
  python examples/mcp/gmail_youtube_demo/after.py

  Verified: ruff clean, both compile, and the preflight message + clean exit
  work. (Note: the trip_demo uses bundled MCP servers, so it runs with no
  external installs ‚ÄĒ just an LLM key ‚ÄĒ if you want a zero-setup MCP example.)

‚Źļ Two separate things here ‚ÄĒ let me explain and fix both.

  Why two ports (7842 and 7843): tracesage defaults to 7842, but if that port is
  already taken it auto-binds the next free one (7843, ‚Ä¶). So a previous
  tracesage server (an earlier after.py/trip_demo/serve you didn't fully stop)
  is still holding 7842, and this run landed on 7843. The real bug is that the
  demo printed a hardcoded 7842 while the tracer actually bound 7843 ‚ÄĒ the
  correct live URL is the ūüĒć tracesage: link (7843). Let me make the demo print
  the actual bound URL.

  The Gmail error is separate: the mcp-google-gmail server needs Google
  Application Default Credentials (not a simple auth command ‚ÄĒ my earlier hint
  was wrong). It failed to load, so the run continued with YouTube only (which
  is why it kept going). Let me fix the printed URL, the misleading Gmail
  guidance, and make Gmail clearly optional.

‚Źļ Now verify everything is clean and commit:

‚Źļ Let me confirm ui_url returns a /ui URL (so the print is correct):

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ ui_url returns the actual bound /ui URL. Everything's clean. Committing:

  Pushed to feat/ui-batch6-split-boxes (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Pushed (3003076). Here's what was going on and what I changed.

  Why two ports ‚ÄĒ not a bug in tracesage

  tracesage binds 7842 by default, and auto-picks the next free port (7843, ‚Ä¶)
  if 7842 is already taken. So you have a stale tracesage server still holding 
  7842 (a previous after.py/trip_demo/serve you didn't fully Ctrl+C), and this
  run landed on 7843.

  The actual bug was cosmetic: the demo printed a hardcoded 7842 banner while
  the run really lived on 7843 (the ūüĒć tracesage: link). Fixed ‚ÄĒ it now prints
  tracer.ui_url, the real bound URL. To free 7842, kill the leftover process:
  lsof -ti :7842 | xargs kill   # then re-runs will use 7842 again

  The Gmail error ‚ÄĒ separate, and Gmail is optional

  mcp-google-gmail authenticates via Google Application Default Credentials
  (google.auth.default()), so it raised DefaultCredentialsError. My earlier
  mcp-google-gmail auth hint was wrong ‚ÄĒ there's no such command. The run
  continued with YouTube only (you can see it fetched the fallback video), which
  is exactly the intended graceful degradation.

  To enable Gmail you need GCP credentials:
  gcloud auth application-default login    # needs gcloud + a GCP project with 
  the Gmail API enabled
  # or: export 
  GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/path/to/oauth-or-service-account.json

  I corrected the docstrings, the preflight message, and the README to say this,
  and marked Gmail optional throughout.

  Two harmless warnings you can ignore

  - LangGraphDeprecatedSinceV10: create_react_agent has been moved‚Ä¶ ‚ÄĒ just a
  deprecation notice; the demo still works.
  - mcp_youtube_transcript ‚Ä¶ starting/closed INFO lines ‚ÄĒ normal server
  lifecycle logging.

  Bottom line: the demo actually ran end-to-end (YouTube path). If you want the
  zero-setup MCP example instead, trip_demo uses bundled servers ‚ÄĒ just an LLM
  key, no external installs or Google auth.

‚Źļ Pushed (efa1333).

  What broke and what I fixed

  The trip demo crashed with Could not resolve authentication method‚Ä¶ api_key
  because no LLM API key was visible ‚ÄĒ and unlike the gmail demo, this script 
  never loaded .env, so the key sitting in your repo's .env was ignored. The
  failure happened deep in the Anthropic client, after already starting three
  MCP servers, hence the giant traceback.

  Three fixes:
  1. Loads .env now (best-effort python-dotenv, same as the gmail demo). Your
  .env already has a key, so just re-run it and it should work.
  2. Key preflight ‚ÄĒ make_llm() checks the provider's key var first and, if
  missing, exits with a clean one-liner instead of a traceback:
  No LLM API key found. This demo defaults to 'anthropic' and needs
  $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
      export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=...
  (Or add it to a .env file in the repo root ‚ÄĒ this script loads .env
  automatically.)
  Use a different provider: export LLM_PROVIDER=openai LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
  OPENAI_API_KEY=...
  2. It runs before any servers/tracer start, so a doomed run no longer spins up
  the MCP processes.
  3. Prints tracer.ui_url (the actually-bound port), not a hardcoded :7842.

  Just re-run it

  python examples/mcp/trip_demo/demo.py
  With the key in .env, the agent will fan out across the flights/weather/hotels
  MCP servers + the local format_travel_brief tool, and the UI link it prints
  will be the correct port.

  (The create_react_agent deprecation warning and the Processing request of type
  ListToolsRequest INFO lines are both normal/harmless.)

‚Źļ Found the real root cause. Look: llm_end events have agent_name = None, while
  chat_model_start events have agent_name = 'ChatOpenAI'. The node label is
  ChatOpenAI, and eventMatchesNode matches LLM events by ev.agent_name === 
  nodeData.label ‚ÄĒ so the token-bearing llm_end events are filtered out entirely
  (their agent_name is null). That's why no tokens show ‚ÄĒ not just in the hero,
  but the per-event badges too.

  The proper fix is in the adapter: carry the model name to the llm_end event.
  Let me find how agent_name/name is set:
  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ There's a _remember_name/_recall_name mechanism (used by chain start/end). The
  LLM start handlers set agent_name but on_llm_end doesn't recall it. Let me
  read the LLM start handlers:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

  recall it in on_llm_end/on_llm_error. Let me check on_llm_error too:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Both halves of the bug are now fixed and verified: llm_end events carry
  agent_name='ChatOpenAI' and tokens (312/83, 853/56, ‚Ä¶), so eventMatchesNode
  will now match them to the LLM node ‚ÄĒ feeding both the per-event token badges

  Pushed to feat/ui-batch6-split-boxes (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Pushed (667f80b). You were right that it should show tokens ‚ÄĒ it was a real
  bug, and a sneaky one.

  Why no tokens showed (it wasn't capture ‚ÄĒ it was attribution)

  Tokens were being recorded correctly in the DB. The problem: the UI matches an
  LLM node's events by agent_name === nodeLabel, and the token counts live on
  llm_end events ‚ÄĒ but only the *_start events had agent_name set. The end
  events came through with agent_name = None, so the matcher filtered out every 
  token-bearing event. The node drawer was left with only the token-less start
  events.

  I confirmed it straight from your DB:
  llm_end | agent_name=None  | token_input=1944 | token_output=423   ‚Üź tokens,
  but no name ‚Üí filtered out
  chat_model_start | agent_name='ChatOpenAI' | None | None           ‚Üź name, but
  no tokens

  The fix (two parts)

  1. Pipeline (adapters/langchain.py): stash the model name on LLM start and
  recall it on on_llm_end/on_llm_error, so end/error events are attributed to
  the same node. Verified after the fix:
  llm_end | agent_name='ChatOpenAI' | 312/83,  853/56,  1123/460,  1995/430
  2. UI (app.js): the node drawer hero now shows Tokens in / Tokens out stats
  plus a "Total tokens: N across M calls" footnote ‚ÄĒ visible immediately,
  without expanding the invocations dropdown. (The per-event token badges in
  that dropdown now appear too, since the end events finally match.)

  Verified: ruff + py_compile clean, node --check on app.js, and
  tests/test_handler.py all 15 pass.

  To see it

  Re-run the demo, click an LLM node in the graph:
  python examples/mcp/trip_demo/demo.py
  The hero will show Tokens in/out. (Note: a run captured before this fix still
  has nameless llm_end rows, so tokens won't show for old runs ‚ÄĒ only new runs.)

      323        this._layoutColumns();

      325      this._applyManualPositions();
      326 +    // A big layout (e.g. many tools) must pan, not shrink eve
          +ry node to a dot.                                             
      327 +    this._clampZoomOut();                                     
      328 +    this._applyViewBox();                                     
      329    }
      330  
      331 +  /** Cap the viewBox so the graph never auto-renders below MI
          +N_FIT_SCALE. Only                                             
      332 +   *  ever zooms IN (shrinks the viewBox); never grows it, and
          + never moves x/y, so                                          
      333 +   *  a user's pan survives data refreshes. Overflow becomes p
          +an/scroll instead of                                          
      334 +   *  everything zooming out into illegibility. The explicit f
          +it() button bypasses                                          
      335 +   *  this (it sets the viewBox directly) so "fit all" can sti
          +ll show everything. */                                        
      336 +  _clampZoomOut() {                                           
      337 +    const cw = this.svg?.clientWidth || 0;                    
      338 +    const ch = this.svg?.clientHeight || 0;                   
      339 +    if (cw < 2 || ch < 2) return;            // pane not laid 
          +out / collapsed                                               
      340 +    const MIN_FIT_SCALE = 0.72;              // nodes never re
          +ndered below ~72%                                             
      341 +    const maxW = cw / MIN_FIT_SCALE;                          
      342 +    const maxH = ch / MIN_FIT_SCALE;                          
      343 +    if (this.viewBox.w > maxW) this.viewBox.w = maxW;         
      344 +    if (this.viewBox.h > maxH) this.viewBox.h = maxH;         
      345 +  }                                                           
      346 +                                                              
      347    /** Re-apply any user-dragged positions on top of the comput
           ed layout (so manual
      348     *  de-cluttering survives topology refreshes). Skips hidden
            (non-participating)
      349     *  nodes so a dragged node does not reappear in a trace it 
           is not part of. */

‚Źļ I dug into your actual run ‚ÄĒ here's exactly what's happening, and it's two
  different things.

  The short answer

  The multiple LLM and tool calls are correct ‚ÄĒ that's your agent's reasoning
  loop, not duplication. What looks like "a start and an end" is each single
  call's request + response pair, which the timeline currently shows as two
  separate rows.

  What your run actually did

  This is a ReAct agent ‚ÄĒ it doesn't call the LLM once, it loops think ‚Üí act ‚Üí 
  observe ‚Üí think again. Your trip-demo run was 5 LLM calls + 7 tool calls:

  ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēī‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēī‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė

  So 5 separate LLM inferences (5 prompts, 5 token bills) and 7 distinct tool
  runs. Collapsing those into "one" would actually be wrong ‚ÄĒ it would hide that
  the agent reasoned 5 times. Each call has exactly one *_start and one *_end;
  there's no double-emission.

  The part that's a display choice

  A single LLM call emits two events ‚ÄĒ chat_model_start (the request) and
  llm_end (the response, carrying duration + tokens). Same for tools (tool_start
  + tool_end). The timeline renders one card per raw event, so a single call
  shows up as two rows. That's the "shouldn't it be one?" you're noticing ‚ÄĒ and
  yes, I can merge each start/end pair into a single entry.

  There's a tradeoff though, and you previously told me you wanted the detailed
  per-event timeline (so nothing gets hidden) ‚ÄĒ so I don't want to guess wrong
  here:

‚Źļ¬†User declined to answer questions
  ‚éŅ ¬†¬∑ How would you like the timeline to show each LLM/tool call? (One row per 
     call / Keep start + end separate / One row per call + hide chain noise)

‚Źļ Now I have the complete, precise picture. Here's the audit result:

  - Timeline cards ‚ÄĒ already correct: a *_start shows only its request summary
  with no token badge; only the *_end card shows tokens, once. ‚úď
  - Backend run-total tokens (worker) ‚ÄĒ sums token_input/output, which exist
  only on llm_end, so each call counts once. ‚úď
  - Backend topology invocation count ‚ÄĒ counts *_start only (ends never bump
  it), so it's correct and unaffected by my change. ‚úď
  - Node drawer ‚ÄĒ this is the actual bug. My recent agent_name fix made both the
  start and end match the LLM node, so the per-run invocation list shows each
  call twice and the count reads 10 instead of 5. (The token sum there stays
  correct only because starts carry null tokens.)

  The fix: route the node drawer through the existing
  groupInvocations/finalizeInvocation helpers (which pair start+end by run_id
  and take tokens from the end only) so each call is one entry, counted once ‚ÄĒ
  for LLMs and tools. Let me implement:

‚Źļ Pushed (953dbd4). Here's the full answer to your question.

  Why you see multiple LLM/tool start+end ‚ÄĒ and what was actually wrong

  The multiple calls are correct. Your trip-demo run is a ReAct agent that loops
  think ‚Üí act ‚Üí observe ‚Üí think again ‚ÄĒ 5 LLM calls + 7 tool calls. Each is a
  genuinely separate inference with its own prompt and token bill; collapsing
  them to "one" would hide the agent's reasoning steps.

  Each call has a *_start (request) and a *_end (response) ‚ÄĒ that's the call's
  lifecycle, where tokens live on the end. That's the two-rows-per-call you
  noticed.

  On your token concern ‚ÄĒ I audited every path

  You were right to flag it. Here's what I found:
  - Timeline cards: already correct ‚ÄĒ a *_start shows only its request summary
  with no token badge; only the *_end shows tokens, once. ‚úď
  - Backend run-total (worker): sums token fields, which exist only on llm_end ‚Üí
  each call once. ‚úď
  - The real bug: my earlier fix (attributing llm_end to the LLM node so tokens
  show) made both the start and end match the node ‚ÄĒ so the node/edge drawer 
  listed each call twice and the invocation count read 10 instead of 5. The
  token sum there stayed correct (starts carry null tokens), but the doubled
  list/count looked exactly like the duplication you were worried about.

  The fix

  Routed the node and edge drawers through the existing pairing helpers
  (groupInvocations/finalizeInvocation), which merge a call's start+end by
  run_id into one logical invocation and take tokens from the end event only.
  Now:
  - One entry per call ‚ÄĒ for LLMs and tools; no standalone *_start row carrying
  response data.
  - The count is the real number of calls (5, not¬†10).
  - Tokens are summed per-invocation, so each call counts exactly once.

  Proof it's not duplicated: the drawer's token total (7,536 in / 816 out)
  matches the backend's independently accumulated run total to the token, and
  the count dropped from 10 raw events ‚Üí 5 logical calls.

  I left the timeline as detailed per-event (start + end rows) since you'd
  previously asked to keep that granularity ‚ÄĒ and there it's already correct
  (start = request, no tokens; end = response + tokens once). If you'd also like
  the timeline collapsed to one row per call, say the word and I'll apply the
  same grouping there.

‚úĽ Cogitated for 8m 6s

‚ĚĮ why does *_start shows response?                                              

‚Źļ Right ‚ÄĒ that's the same issue you flagged, and it's in a different place than
  the count fix: the step drawer (openStepDrawer). It deliberately pairs a
  call's start+end by run_id and renders both "Request payload" and "Response
  payload" no matter which you clicked ‚ÄĒ so clicking a *_start card shows the
  Response too. Let me read it and gate the response on the clicked phase:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

  completed call ‚ÄĒ which is what the merged invocation cards open). Let me edit:

  Ran 2 shell commands (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Classification is correct ‚ÄĒ every *_start opens to request only, every
  *_end/*_error shows the full call. Let me commit:

  Pushed to feat/ui-batch6-split-boxes (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Pushed (9349dbe).

  Why a *_start was showing a response

  It was the step drawer (openStepDrawer) ‚ÄĒ a different spot from the count fix.
  By design it paired a call's two halves by run_id and rendered both a
  "Request payload" and a "Response payload" section no matter which card you 
  clicked. So clicking a chat_model_start (or tool_start/chain_start) pulled in
  the response from its paired *_end and showed it ‚ÄĒ exactly the conflation you
  flagged.

  The fix

  The clicked event's phase now decides what's shown:
  - *_start / run_start / agent_action (a request) ‚Üí Request payload only. No
  response section, and no token line (tokens live on the end).
  - *_end / *_error (a response) ‚Üí Request (as context) + Response ‚ÄĒ the full
  completed call. This is also what the merged invocation cards in the node/edge
  drawer open, so clicking a call there still shows both halves.

  Verified the phase classification for every event type: every *_start resolves
  to request-only, every *_end/*_error to the full call.

  So now the start/end distinction is honest end-to-end:
  - Timeline *_start card ‚Üí click ‚Üí request only.
  - Timeline *_end card ‚Üí click ‚Üí request + response.
  - Node/edge drawer ‚Üí one row per call ‚Üí click ‚Üí full request + response,
  tokens counted once.

      12 +## [0.3.0] ‚ÄĒ 2026-06-23                                        
      13 +                                                               
      14 +A major UI overhaul (topology vs. run-trace, step-through repla
         +y, readable                                                    
      15 +multi-server graphs), two real-world MCP example apps, and accu
         +rate per-call                                                  
      16 +token attribution.                                             
      17 +                                                               
      18 +### Added                                                      
      19 +- **Topology vs. Run-trace views.** A single graph pane now tog
         +gles between two                                               
      20 +  layouts: **Topology** (the all-up system architecture, nodes 
         +in per-kind                                                    
      21 +  columns) and **Run trace** (one selected run laid out as a le
         +ft ‚Üí right call                                                
      22 +  tree in call order). Selecting a run opens its trace; the Top
         +ology button                                                   
      23 +  returns to the architecture view.                            
      24 +- **Step-through replay.** Explicit **Start / Pause / Resume** 
         +controls plus                                                  
      25 +  **Prev / Next** manual stepping walk a run's execution on the
         + graph. Start                                                  
      26 +  always restarts from the beginning; Resume only works once pa
         +used. Clicking a                                               
      27 +  timeline step during replay pauses and jumps the cursor to th
         +at event.                                                      
      28 +- **Run-status toasts.** The UI pops a toast when a run you're 
         +watching finishes ‚ÄĒ                                            
      29 +  "Run completed" (or "Run failed: ‚Ä¶" with the error) ‚ÄĒ driven 
         +by the `/ws/runs`                                              
      30 +  lifecycle feed, so you don't have to keep eyes on the run lis
         +t.                                                             
      31 +- **Per-call token usage on LLM nodes.** Open an LLM node to se
         +e total **Tokens                                               
      32 +  in / Tokens out** and a "Total tokens across N calls" line in
         + the hero, summed                                              
      33 +  per logical call (counted once, from each call's end event). 
      34 +- **Gmail + YouTube demo** (`examples/mcp/gmail_youtube_demo/`)
         + ‚ÄĒ a ReAct agent                                               
      35 +  that reads a real Gmail inbox, pulls YouTube transcripts from
         + linked videos, and                                            
      36 +  summarises them. Ships `before.py` / `after.py` so the exact 
         +tracesage diff (two                                            
      37 +  imports, two setup lines, one `callbacks=` kwarg) is obvious.
         + Gmail is optional                                             
      38 +  (needs Google Application Default Credentials); without it th
         +e agent runs with                                              
      39 +  YouTube only. `credentials.json` / `token.json` are gitignore
         +d.                                                             
      40 +- **Trip Planner demo** (`examples/mcp/trip_demo/`) ‚ÄĒ a single 
         +agent planning a trip                                          
      41 +  across **three** MCP servers (flights, weather, hotels, 7 too
         +ls each) plus a local                                          
      42 +  formatting tool, showcasing per-server tool-source attributio
         +n in the topology and                                          
      43 +  the "Tools by source" panel. Bundles the three stdio MCP serv
         +ers ‚ÄĒ no external                                              
      44 +  installs, just an LLM key. Loads a repo-root `.env` automatic
         +ally.                                                          
      45 +- `examples/mcp/requirements.txt` pinning the demos' dependenci
         +es.                                                            
      46 +                                                               
      47 +### Changed                                                    
      48 +- **Readable graphs at scale.** MCP servers get distinct colour
         +s; a server's tools                                            
      49 +  are grouped and tinted by source. A large tool fan-out wraps 
         +into multiple                                                  
      50 +  sub-columns, and the auto-layout no longer shrinks every node
         + to fit ‚ÄĒ it caps                                              
      51 +  the zoom (~72% floor) and pans/scrolls instead, so nodes stay
         + legible. The                                                  
      52 +  explicit "fit" button still zooms to show everything.        
      53 +- **Detailed timeline + node-drawer invocations.** The timeline
         + keeps one card per                                            
      54 +  event; node and edge drawers now group a call's start (reques
         +t) + end (response)                                            
      55 +  into **one logical invocation**, so counts and token totals a
         +re never doubled.                                              
      56 +- **Step drawer is phase-aware.** Opening a `*_start` shows onl
         +y its **request**;                                             
      57 +  opening a `*_end` / `*_error` shows the full completed call (
         +request + response).                                           
      58 +- **Connection liveness.** The `/ws/runs` feed sends periodic h
         +eartbeats and the                                              
      59 +  client tracks liveness, so the header reflects a dropped conn
         +ection instead of                                              
      60 +  showing a stale "connected".                                 
      61 +- UI polish: plain `START` / `END` flags (no glyphs), full run 
         +id shown beside the                                            
      62 +  Timeline heading, full-height collapse gutters mirroring the 
         +expand rails, and                                              
      63 +  the topology-scope selector + "Tools by source" panel hidden 
         +in Run-trace mode.                                             
      64 +                                                               
      65 +### Fixed                                                      
      66 +- **Token attribution.** `llm_end` events now carry the model n
         +ame, so token usage                                            
      67 +  (which lives on the end event) is attributed to the LLM node 
         +‚ÄĒ previously the                                               
      68 +  end events were nameless and dropped from the node's invocati
         +on set.                                                        
      69 +- **Demo ergonomics.** Both MCP demos print the **actual** boun
         +d UI port (auto-port                                           
      70 +  may pick 7843+ if 7842 is busy), preflight their prerequisite
         +s (MCP servers / LLM                                           
      71 +  API key) and exit with clear setup instructions instead of cr
         +yptic failures.                                                
      72 +

      327    timeline quickly without opening anything.
      328  - **Request / Response payloads** ‚ÄĒ the *full* data, stored as
            gzipped blobs.
      329    A step pairs its `*_start` event (the **request**: inputs / 
           prompt / query)
      330 -  with its `*_end` event (the **response**: outputs / result).
          - Click any step                                               
      331 -  in the UI and the drawer shows both, side by side, plus toke
          -ns, duration, and                                             
      332 -  any error.                                                  
      330 +  with its `*_end` event (the **response**: outputs / result).
          + Opening a `*_start`                                          
      331 +  shows just the request; opening a `*_end` shows the full req
          +uest **and** response,                                        
      332 +  plus tokens, duration, and any error.

‚Źļ Done. I recorded a full demo of the v0.3.0 UI and wired the new video +
  screenshots into the docs.

  The demo

  Recorded a scripted walkthrough on a real Trip Planner run (3 MCP servers, 21
  tools) covering, in order:
  1. Topology graph (per-kind columns, distinct per-server tool colours, legend)
  2. Tools by source panel
  3. Selecting a run ‚Üí Run-trace call tree
  4. Step-through replay at 5√ó ‚ÄĒ the graph animating with a live "REPLAY STEP
  17/66" badge while the timeline logs highlight (durations, tool calls)
  5. A step's full request / response payload
  6. The LLM node token usage (in 7,497 / out 777, total 8,274 across 5 calls,
  per-call breakdown)
  7. The MCP server inspector

  How (no system ffmpeg needed): headless Chrome via CDP captured frames at
  ~10fps while a scheduled action map drove the tour; encoded with the ffmpeg
  bundled in imageio-ffmpeg.

  Assets produced

  README "watch a trace stream" thumbnail (autoplays inline on GitHub, links to
  the full clip). This fixes the previously-broken gif reference.

      320  
      321  ### CLI
      322  
      323 +The `tracesage` command ships with the package ‚ÄĒ a **viewer + 
          +utilities** over a                                            
      324 +data directory. It never ingests events; ingestion happens onl
          +y when your code calls                                        
      325 +`TraceSage.create()`. The fastest way to see it working, with 
          +zero code:                                                    
      326 +                                                              
      327  ```bash
      324 -tracesage serve  --data-dir ~/.tracesage          # read-only 
          -viewer                                                        
      325 -tracesage export --run-id RUN_ID -o trace.jsonl   # export to 
          -JSONL                                                         
      326 -tracesage import -i trace.jsonl                   # import a J
          -SONL export                                                   
      327 -tracesage stats  --data-dir ~/.tracesage          # summary st
          -ats                                                           
      328 -tracesage runs   --status failed --limit 20       # list runs 
      329 -tracesage gc     --max-runs 10000                 # retention 
          -cleanup                                                       
      330 -tracesage doctor --data-dir ~/.tracesage          # data-dir d
          -iagnostics                                                    
      331 -tracesage version                                             
      328 +tracesage demo                                    # seed a sam
          +ple trace, serve + open the UI                                
      329  ```
      330  
      334 -See [`docs/cli.md`](docs/cli.md) for full reference.          
      331 +**Inspect traces in the browser:**                            
      332  
      333 +| Command | What it does |                                    
      334 +|---|---|                                                     
      335 +| `tracesage serve -d ~/.tracesage [--open]` | Read-only viewe
          +r over an existing data dir (auto-picks the next free port; `-
          +-auth-token` to gate it) |                                    
      336 +| `tracesage view trace.jsonl [--open]` | Open an exported JSO
          +NL trace directly in the UI |                                 
      337 +                                                              
      338 +**Inspect traces in the terminal (no server):**               
      339 +                                                              
      340 +| Command | What it does |                                    
      341 +|---|---|                                                     
      342 +| `tracesage runs --status failed --limit 20` | List root runs
          + (filter by `--status` / `--tag`, `--json` for NDJSON) |      
      343 +| `tracesage show <run_id>` | Render a run as a colour-coded i
          +ndented call tree (MCP tools tagged `mcp:<server>`) |         
      344 +| `tracesage watch <run_id>` | Live-tail a run's events as the
          +y're written (`--once` to print and exit) |                   
      345 +| `tracesage diff <run_a> <run_b>` | Compare two runs side by 
          +side ‚ÄĒ status, steps, tokens, tools, errors |                 
      346 +| `tracesage stats [--json]` | Summary stats ‚ÄĒ run counts by s
          +tatus, avg duration, token totals, DB size |                  
      347 +                                                              
      348 +**Move, retain, diagnose:**                                   
      349 +                                                              
      350 +| Command | What it does |                                    
      351 +|---|---|                                                     
      352 +| `tracesage export [RUN_ID] --all -o trace.jsonl` | Dump one 
          +run or `--all` to JSONL (`-o -` for stdout) |                 
      353 +| `tracesage import -i trace.jsonl -d DIR` | Read a JSONL expo
          +rt back into a data dir (backups / move between machines) |   
      354 +| `tracesage gc --max-runs 10000 [--dry-run]` | Retention ‚ÄĒ de
          +lete oldest runs/blobs beyond a count or `--max-blob-size-gb` 
          +|                                                             
      355 +| `tracesage doctor -d ~/.tracesage` | Read-only diagnostics ‚ÄĒ
          + schema version, run counts, orphan/missing-blob checks |     
      356 +| `tracesage version` | Print the installed version |         
      357 +                                                              
      358 +Most commands take `--data-dir, -d` (default `~/.tracesage`). 
          +See                                                           
      359 +[`docs/cli.md`](docs/cli.md) for the full per-command referenc
          +e.

‚úĽ Saut√©ed for 1m 27s

‚ĚĮ now in the demo I've to explain this particular usecase, give me short        
  description of explanation of this usecase                                    

‚Źļ Here's a short explainer you can use when presenting the trip-planner usecase:

  The usecase ‚ÄĒ Trip Planner agent

  ‚Ėé One AI agent plans a weekend trip from NYC to Tokyo by orchestrating three 
  ‚Ėé independent MCP servers.

  A user asks a single natural-language question:

  ‚Ėé "Plan a weekend trip from NYC to Tokyo ‚ÄĒ find a good flight and its baggage 
  ‚Ėé policy, check Tokyo's weather and 7-day forecast, find a best-value hotel, 
  ‚Ėé and write me a travel brief."

  A single ReAct agent decides, on its own, which tools to call and in what
  order. The tools live across three separate MCP servers plus one local
  function:

  - ‚úąÔłŹ Flights server ‚ÄĒ search flights, baggage policy, fares, seat maps‚Ä¶ (7
  tools)
  - ūüĆ§ÔłŹ Weather server ‚ÄĒ current conditions, 7-day forecast, air quality‚Ä¶ (7
  tools)
  - ūüŹ® Hotels server ‚ÄĒ search, details, rates, cancellation policy‚Ä¶ (7 tools)
  - ūüõ†ÔłŹ Local tool ‚ÄĒ format_travel_brief (your own code, not an MCP server)

  The agent loops ‚ÄĒ reason ‚Üí call tools ‚Üí read results ‚Üí reason again ‚ÄĒ until it
  has enough to produce the final brief. In this run that's 5 LLM calls and 7 
  tool calls spread across all three servers.

  Why it's the right demo

  This is exactly where observability hurts most: multiple tool sources, a 
  non-deterministic agent loop, and tools you didn't write. When the answer is
  wrong or slow, you need to know which server returned what, in what order, and
  how many tokens it cost ‚ÄĒ and that's invisible from the terminal.

  That's the before ‚Üí after story:
  - before.py ‚ÄĒ same agent, no tracesage. You get a silent run and a final
  paragraph. "Which of the 21 tools fired? What did each return? How many 
  tokens? Where did the time go?" ‚ÄĒ unanswerable.
  - demo.py ‚ÄĒ same agent + a minimal tracesage change. Every question answered
  live in the browser: topology of agent‚Üíservers‚Üítools, the run-trace timeline,
  per-tool request/response payloads, and token usage per LLM call.

  One-liner if you need it: "A trip-planning agent that fans out across three 
  MCP servers ‚ÄĒ the kind of multi-tool, multi-step agent that's a nightmare to 
  debug blind, and trivial to debug with tracesage."

  Want me to tailor this to a specific audience (e.g. engineers vs.
  decision-makers) or tighten it to a 30-second script?

‚úĽ Baked for 18s

‚ĚĮ give me whole script on how to introduce this thing the need of it from hi    
  everyone, to before and after of the trip demo application. then I'll pick it 
  up                                                                            

‚Źļ Here's a complete spoken script ‚ÄĒ from "hi everyone" through the need, then
  the before/after demo. Stage directions are in (italics); everything else is
  meant to be said aloud.

  ---
  tracesage demo script

  1. Open ‚ÄĒ hook (‚Čą30 sec)

  ‚Ėé "Hi everyone, thanks for being here.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Quick show of hands ‚ÄĒ how many of you have built something with an AI agent?
  ‚Ėé And how many of you have had that agent do something weird, and had no idea
  ‚Ėé why?
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé (pause)
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé That second problem ‚ÄĒ not knowing why your agent did what it did ‚ÄĒ is what I
  ‚Ėé want to talk about today. I'll show you the pain in about sixty seconds, 
  ‚Ėé and then I'll show you a tool that makes it disappear. It's called 
  ‚Ėé tracesage."

  ---
  2. The need ‚ÄĒ why agents are hard to debug (‚Čą1 min)

  ‚Ėé "Here's the thing about modern AI agents. A few years ago, a program did 
  ‚Ėé exactly what you wrote ‚ÄĒ step one, step two, step three. You could read the 
  ‚Ėé code and know what happened.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Agents don't work like that. You give an agent a goal and a set of tools, 
  ‚Ėé and the agent decides what to call, in what order, how many times. Same 
  ‚Ėé input can take a different path every run.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé And it gets worse with MCP ‚ÄĒ the Model Context Protocol. MCP lets your agent
  ‚Ėé plug into tools it didn't write ‚ÄĒ a flights service, a weather service, a 
  ‚Ėé database, somebody else's API. Great for capability. Terrible for debugging.
  ‚Ėé Because now when something goes wrong, you're asking:
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé - Which tools actually fired?
  ‚Ėé - What did each one return?
  ‚Ėé - Did the model misread a correct result, or did a tool give it bad data?
  ‚Ėé - How many LLM calls did this take? How many tokens? Where did the time go?
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Today, the honest answer to all of those is: you add print statements and 
  ‚Ėé run it again, and hope it breaks the same way. Let me show you exactly what 
  ‚Ėé I mean."

  ---
  3. The "before" ‚ÄĒ run it blind (‚Čą1.5 min)

  (Switch to terminal. Have before.py ready to run.)

  ‚Ėé "This is a real agent. The usecase is a trip planner. I ask it one question 
  ‚Ėé in plain English ‚ÄĒ
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé 'Plan a weekend trip from NYC to Tokyo ‚ÄĒ find me a flight and its baggage 
  ‚Ėé policy, check the weather and the 7-day forecast, find a good hotel, and 
  ‚Ėé write me a travel brief.'
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Behind the scenes, one agent is orchestrating three separate MCP servers ‚ÄĒ 
  ‚Ėé flights, weather, and hotels ‚ÄĒ plus one of my own tools to format the brief.
  ‚Ėé That's twenty-two tools it can choose from.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Let's run it ‚ÄĒ with no observability, the way most people run agents today."

  (Run: python examples/mcp/trip_demo/before.py)

  ‚Ėé "And now‚Ä¶ we wait. (let the silent run sit for a beat) Notice there's 
  ‚Ėé nothing to look at. No idea what it's doing. Just a blinking cursor."

  (Output appears ‚ÄĒ the travel brief.)

  ‚Ėé "Okay ‚ÄĒ it worked. We got a travel brief. Flight, weather, hotel. Looks 
  ‚Ėé great.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé But here's my question: how do I know it's right? Let me ask you what I 
  ‚Ėé can't tell from this screen:
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé - Did it actually check all three servers, or did it skip one and make 
  ‚Ėé something up?
  ‚Ėé - It found this hotel ‚ÄĒ did it compare the others first, or grab the first 
  ‚Ėé result?
  ‚Ėé - This took a few seconds and cost me tokens ‚ÄĒ how many LLM calls? Was the 
  ‚Ėé weather server slow, or the model?
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé I can't answer any of that. All I have is a paragraph of text and a pile of 
  ‚Ėé trust. And if that brief had been wrong ‚ÄĒ wrong baggage fee, stale forecast 
  ‚Ėé ‚ÄĒ I'd have no idea where it broke. That's the reality of shipping agents 
  ‚Ėé blind."

  ---
  4. The "after" ‚ÄĒ same agent, with tracesage (‚Čą2.5 min)

  (Switch back to terminal / editor.)

  ‚Ėé "Now here's the fun part. I have a second version of this exact same script.
  ‚Ėé Same agent, same question, same three MCP servers, same model. The only 
  ‚Ėé difference is I've added tracesage ‚ÄĒ a minimal change, just a couple of 
  ‚Ėé imports and one line on the call.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé No rewrite. No re-architecting. I'm not changing my agent ‚ÄĒ I'm just 
  ‚Ėé watching it."

  (Run: python examples/mcp/trip_demo/demo.py --open)

  ‚Ėé "Same run as before ‚ÄĒ but this time it opens a UI in my browser, running 
  ‚Ėé entirely on my machine. Let's walk through it."

  Topology (point at the graph)

  ‚Ėé "First, the topology. This is my whole system at a glance. One agent, 
  ‚Ėé fanning out to three MCP servers ‚ÄĒ flights in pink, hotels, weather ‚ÄĒ each 
  ‚Ėé colour-coded, each with its tools hanging off it. I can see the shape of my 
  ‚Ėé application. Twenty-one MCP tools plus my local one, all attributed to where
  ‚Ėé they came from ‚ÄĒ automatically."

  Run-trace + timeline (click the run)

  ‚Ėé "Now let me click into the actual run. The graph flips to a run-trace ‚ÄĒ the 
  ‚Ėé exact path this run took, left to right, in call order. And on the right is 
  ‚Ėé the timeline ‚ÄĒ every single step, with timing.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé (scroll the timeline) There it is: the agent called search_flights, then 
  ‚Ėé get_baggage_policy, then the weather tools, then hotels ‚ÄĒ I can see the real
  ‚Ėé order it happened in, and how long each took."

  Replay (hit Start on replay)

  ‚Ėé "And I can replay it ‚ÄĒ watch the run animate step by step, like a debugger 
  ‚Ėé for your agent's reasoning. (let it play a second, then pause)"

  Step payload (click a tool step)

  ‚Ėé "Here's the part that kills the guesswork. I click any step ‚ÄĒ and I get the 
  ‚Ėé full request and response. This is exactly what went into the tool and 
  ‚Ėé exactly what came back. So if the answer's ever wrong, I don't guess ‚ÄĒ I 
  ‚Ėé look. Was it a bad tool result, or did the model ignore a good one? Now I 
  ‚Ėé can tell, in one click."

  Tokens (click the LLM node)

  ‚Ėé "And cost and performance ‚ÄĒ click the LLM node: tokens in, tokens out, total
  ‚Ėé across every call in the run, plus latency. Five LLM calls, 
  ‚Ėé eighty-three-hundred tokens, right there. No more wondering what a run cost 
  ‚Ėé me."

  ---
  5. Close ‚ÄĒ the payoff (‚Čą30 sec)

  ‚Ėé "So ‚ÄĒ same agent, both times. The only difference was a few lines of code.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé (beat)
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé Without it: a paragraph of text and a lot of hope. With it: I can see every 
  ‚Ėé tool that fired, what it returned, how long it took, and what it cost ‚ÄĒ for 
  ‚Ėé any run, including ones using tools I didn't even write.
  ‚Ėé
  ‚Ėé That's tracesage. It's local-first, it's open source, and you can drop it 
  ‚Ėé into an existing agent in about a minute. Let me show you how to get started
  ‚Ėé ‚ÄĒ (transition to install / Q&A)."

  ---
  Quick reference ‚ÄĒ run order & cues

  ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ē¨‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź
  ‚Ēā Moment ‚Ēā                        Command / action                        ‚Ēā
  ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēľ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ē§
  ‚Ēā Before ‚Ēā python examples/mcp/trip_demo/before.py                        ‚Ēā
  ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēľ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ē§
  ‚Ēā After  ‚Ēā python examples/mcp/trip_demo/demo.py --open                   ‚Ēā

  ‚Ēā UI     ‚Ēā Topology ‚Üí click run (run-trace + timeline) ‚Üí Start replay ‚Üí   ‚Ēā
  ‚Ēā beats  ‚Ēā click a tool step (payload) ‚Üí click LLM node (tokens)          ‚Ēā
  ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēī‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė

  Two safety tips for the live run:
  - Pre-run demo.py once before the talk so the trip data is already in
  ~/.tracesage/trip-demo ‚ÄĒ then if the live run is slow or the network hiccups,
  the UI still has a run to explore.
  - Make sure your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (or other key) is in .env, and free up port
  7842 (lsof -ti :7842 | xargs kill) so the URL it prints is the clean default.

  Want me to cut this down to a tight 3-minute version, or adjust the tone for a
  specific audience (engineers vs. execs)?

‚úĽ Churned for 44s

‚ĚĮ this is a demo video not some presentation, and give me in proper script      

‚Źļ Got it ‚ÄĒ a voiceover script for a screen-recorded demo video. No audience, no
  "raise your hands." Just narration timed to what's on screen. Here it is.

  ---
  tracesage ‚ÄĒ Trip Planner Demo (video script)

  Format: voiceover over screen recording ‚ÄĘ Target length: ~3.5 min
  Layout: [SCREEN] = what's recording ‚ÄĘ VO: = what you say

  ---
  Scene 1 ‚ÄĒ Cold open / the problem (~20s)

  [SCREEN: editor showing the trip_demo agent code, or a plain terminal]

  VO:
  "This is an AI agent that plans a trip. You give it one instruction in plain
  English, and it figures out the rest ‚ÄĒ which tools to call, in what order, how
  many times.

  The problem is, when it does something wrong, you have no idea why. Let me
  show you."

  ---
  Scene 2 ‚ÄĒ The "before" run (~50s)

  [SCREEN: terminal, clean]

  VO:
  "Here's the agent. It plans a weekend trip from New York to Tokyo. Behind the
  scenes it's orchestrating three separate MCP servers ‚ÄĒ flights, weather, and
  hotels ‚ÄĒ plus one tool of my own to write the final brief. That's over twenty
  tools it can choose from.

  Let's run it the way most agents run today ‚ÄĒ with no observability."

  [SCREEN: type and run]
  python examples/mcp/trip_demo/before.py

  [SCREEN: silent run, cursor blinking ‚ÄĒ let it sit ~3s]

  VO:
  "And now we wait. Nothing to see. No idea what it's doing."

  [SCREEN: the travel brief prints out]

  VO:
  "And there's our answer ‚ÄĒ a travel brief. Flight, weather, hotel. Looks good.

  But here's the problem: I can't tell if it's right. Did it actually check all
  three servers, or skip one and guess? Did it compare hotels, or grab the
  first? How many model calls did this take ‚ÄĒ how many tokens? If this brief
  were wrong, I'd have no idea where it broke.

  All I have is text, and trust."

  ---
  Scene 3 ‚ÄĒ The "after" setup (~25s)

  [SCREEN: editor ‚ÄĒ show demo.py, briefly highlight the tracesage lines]

  VO:
  "Now here's the same agent ‚ÄĒ same question, same three servers, same model.
  The only difference is I've added tracesage. A couple of imports and one line
  on the call. I'm not changing how the agent works ‚ÄĒ I'm just watching it.

  Let's run this version."

  [SCREEN: terminal]
  python examples/mcp/trip_demo/demo.py --open

  [SCREEN: browser opens to the tracesage UI]

  VO:
  "Same run ‚ÄĒ but this time it opens a UI in my browser, running entirely on my
  machine."

  ---
  Scene 4 ‚ÄĒ Topology (~30s)

  [SCREEN: topology graph, full view]

  VO:
  "First, the topology. This is my entire system at a glance. One agent, fanning
  out to three MCP servers ‚ÄĒ flights, hotels, weather ‚ÄĒ each color-coded, each
  with its tools branching off.

  Every tool is automatically attributed to the server it came from. Twenty-one
  tools across three servers, plus my own ‚ÄĒ and I didn't label any of this by
  hand."

  ---
  Scene 5 ‚ÄĒ Run trace + timeline (~30s)

  [SCREEN: click the run in the left list ‚Üí graph switches to run-trace; 
  timeline fills on the right]

  VO:
  "Now I'll click into the actual run. The graph flips to a run-trace ‚ÄĒ the
  exact path this run took, left to right, in the order things happened.

  And on the right, the timeline: every single step, with timing. I can see it
  called search_flights, then the baggage policy, then weather, then hotels ‚ÄĒ
  the real sequence, and how long each one took."

  ---
  Scene 6 ‚ÄĒ Replay (~20s)

  [SCREEN: click Start on replay controls ‚Üí let the graph animate ~4s ‚Üí Pause]

  VO:
  "I can replay the whole thing ‚ÄĒ watch the run light up step by step, like a
  debugger for the agent's reasoning. Pause it anywhere."

  ---
  Scene 7 ‚ÄĒ Step payload (the money shot) (~30s)

  [SCREEN: click a tool step in the timeline ‚Üí drawer opens with request + 
  response]

  VO:
  "And here's what kills the guesswork. I click any step, and I get the full
  request and response ‚ÄĒ exactly what went into the tool, and exactly what came
  back.

  So if an answer is ever wrong, I don't guess. I look. Was it a bad tool
  result, or did the model ignore a good one? Now I can tell ‚ÄĒ in one click."

  ---
  Scene 8 ‚ÄĒ Tokens / cost (~20s)

  [SCREEN: switch to Topology, click the LLM node ‚Üí drawer shows token usage]

  VO:
  "And cost. I click the LLM node ‚ÄĒ tokens in, tokens out, total across every
  call in the run, plus latency. Right here. No more wondering what a run
  actually cost me."

  ---
  Scene 9 ‚ÄĒ Close (~20s)

  [SCREEN: pull back to the full topology, or the run-trace]

  VO:
  "Same agent, both times. The only difference was a few lines of code.

  Without it: a paragraph of text and a lot of hope. With it: I can see every
  tool that fired, what it returned, how long it took, and what it cost ‚ÄĒ for
  any run, even using tools I didn't write.

  That's tracesage. Local-first, open source, and you can drop it into an
  existing agent in about a minute."

  [SCREEN: optional end card ‚ÄĒ logo + repo URL]

  ---
  Recording checklist (not narrated)

‚úĽ Saut√©ed for 35s

‚ĚĮ you didn't had the hi everyone and all those things add them see this is a

‚Źļ Got it ‚ÄĒ you want the full spoken script with the greeting and intro, that
  you'll read start to finish. Here it is.

  ---
  tracesage ‚ÄĒ Trip Planner Demo (full video script)

  Read this top to bottom. Stage cues are in [brackets]; everything else is 
  spoken.

  ---
  VO:
  "Hi everyone! In this video I want to show you a tool I've been working on
  called tracesage ‚ÄĒ and more importantly, the problem it solves.

  If you've ever built something with an AI agent, you've probably hit this: the
  agent does something, you're not sure why, and you have no real way to look
  inside and find out. That's what I want to fix today. I'll show you the pain
  first, then I'll show you how tracesage makes it disappear."

  ---
  VO:
  "So let's talk about why agents are hard to debug.

  Regular code does exactly what you wrote ‚ÄĒ step one, step two, step three. You
  can read it and know what happened. Agents don't work that way. You give an
  agent a goal and a set of tools, and the agent decides what to call, in what
  order, and how many times. The same input can take a different path every
  single run.

  And it gets harder with MCP ‚ÄĒ the Model Context Protocol ‚ÄĒ which lets your
  agent use tools it didn't even write: somebody else's flights service, a
  weather API, a database. Powerful, but now when something breaks, you're
  asking: which tools actually fired? What did each one return? How many model
  calls did it take? How many tokens? And the honest answer today is usually ‚ÄĒ
  add some print statements and run it again.

  Let me show you exactly what that feels like."

  ---
  [Switch to terminal ‚ÄĒ clean]

  VO:
  "Here's a real agent. It's a trip planner ‚ÄĒ I ask it one question in plain
  English: plan a weekend trip from New York to Tokyo, find a flight and its
  baggage policy, check the weather and the forecast, find a good hotel, and
  write me a travel brief.

  Behind the scenes, one agent is orchestrating three separate MCP servers ‚ÄĒ
  flights, weather, and hotels ‚ÄĒ plus one tool of my own to format the brief.
  That's over twenty tools it can choose from.

  Let's run it the way most people run agents today ‚ÄĒ with no observability."

  [Run: python examples/mcp/trip_demo/before.py]
  [Let the silent run sit for ~3 seconds]

  VO:
  "And now‚Ä¶ we wait. Notice there's nothing to look at. No idea what it's doing.
  Just a blinking cursor."

  [The travel brief prints]

  VO:
  "Okay ‚ÄĒ it worked. We got a travel brief. Flight, weather, hotel. Looks great.

  But here's my question: how do I know it's right? Look at what I can't tell
  from this screen. Did it actually check all three servers, or skip one and
  make something up? Did it compare hotels, or just grab the first result? How
  many model calls did this take, and how many tokens did it cost? And if this
  brief had been wrong ‚ÄĒ wrong baggage fee, stale forecast ‚ÄĒ I'd have no idea
  where it broke.

  All I have is a paragraph of text and a pile of trust. That's the reality of
  running agents blind."

  ---
  [Switch to the editor ‚ÄĒ show demo.py, highlight the tracesage lines]

  VO:
  "Now here's the fun part. I've got a second version of this exact same script.

  imports and one line on the call.

  came back.

‚úĽ Worked for 1h 21m 57s

‚ĚĮ consider that this demo is for YC startup, give me answers for those          
  questions based on YC history                                                 

‚Źļ Good framing ‚ÄĒ YC's whole ethos is "make something people want" and show the 
  real thing, not a slick fake. Launch YC videos and Demo Day pitches reward
  authentic, fast, real-product footage over over-produced motion graphics. Here
  are decisive answers in that spirit:

  Decisions ‚ÄĒ answered the YC way

  1. Beat 7 (reveal): live browser paint-in ‚ÄĒ REAL.
  This is the single most YC-aligned call. The whole video pivots on "the agent
  is a black box ‚Üí now you can see inside." Faking the reveal with an animated
  PNG undercuts the one moment that has to feel genuine. Record the actual
  blank-tab ‚Üí 127.0.0.1:7842 ‚Üí paint-in + connecting‚Üíconnected chip. Real 
  product reveal = the YC money shot.

  2. Beat 10 (step inspect): re-capture a live drawer slide-in.
  Same logic. A static PNG reads as a screenshot; a live
  click‚Üídrawer-slides-in‚Üízoom-on-tokens reads as "this is a working tool I can
  poke at right now." YC reviewers can smell a mockup. Capture it live. (Keep
  ui-step-payload.png only as a fallback if the live capture fights me.)

  3. Run consistency: yes ‚ÄĒ pin ONE real run, hard.
  Nothing screams "staged" louder than the token count being 816/83 in one shot
  and different in the next. Re-run demo.py once, lock that run id, and drive
  every UI beat (7‚Äď11) from it. Coherent real numbers = credibility. This is
  non-negotiable for a YC audience that scrutinizes whether the demo is real.

  4. Beat 6 (diff): GitHub-style red/green hunks.
  YC's audience is technical ‚ÄĒ founders, engineers, the HN crowd. A real-looking
  git diff says "this is a 1-line integration into code you already have." A
  stylized before/after card says "marketing." Use the authentic diff; it
  doubles as proof the integration is trivial.

  5. Music: light ambient bed ‚ÄĒ yes, but understated.
  Pure silence on a silent (no-VO) video feels broken/unfinished, and YC launch
  videos almost always carry a low, unobtrusive track. But keep it quiet and let
  it duck under the two dwell moments (beat 3's "‚Ä¶running‚Ä¶" silence, beat 7's
  reveal hold) so the product stays the star. Not energetic startup-promo music
  ‚ÄĒ calm, confident.

  6. Beat 4 brief text: accept the live run's real output, pinned.
  Per decision 3 ‚ÄĒ capture beat 3 once, reuse that exact frame for beat 4. Don't
  hand-write a "prettier" brief. The slightly-imperfect real LLM output is more
  convincing to YC than a polished fake, and reusing the same frame keeps 3‚Üí4
  continuous.

  The throughline

  Every answer lands on the same YC principle: maximize realness, minimize 
  theater. Track A's motion graphics carry the narrative (hook, questions,
  transitions, close); Track B stays 100% real product footage ‚ÄĒ no faked UI,
  one coherent run, an honest diff. That contrast is exactly what makes a
  technical YC demo land.

  ---

  Claude Design and I work from the locked spec), and then immediately pin the 
  run ‚ÄĒ re-run demo.py, capture the real run id, and update beats 7‚Äď11 to
  reference it? That's the natural next "plan by plan" step before any capture.

         + shrunk to fit.** Every beat fills the frame with the *one elem
         +ent that matters* ‚ÄĒ the relevant node cluster, drawer, code lin
         +e, or terminal block ‚ÄĒ cropped/zoomed so text is comfortably re
         +adable at 1080p (target: smallest meaningful text ‚Č• 24px on the
         + 1080 canvas). Wide context shots only appear briefly as the *s
         +tart* of a push-in, then settle tight. When in doubt, crop tigh
         +ter. Capture at devicePixelRatio 2 so a 2‚Äď3√ó crop stays sharp.

      104  
      105  **Global capture settings:** viewport 1600√ó1000, devicePixelRa
           tio 2, dark theme, headless Chrome via CDP (per memory `ui-scr
           eenshot-workflow`). Server binds `127.0.0.1:7842`. Populate DB

      106  
      107 +**Capture for ZOOM (applies to every Track B beat):** the fram
          +ing principle (¬ß1) means each capture must yield a crop tight 
          +enough that text is ‚Č•24px on the 1080 canvas. To get there wit
          +hout blur: (a) capture at **devicePixelRatio 2** so a 2‚Äď3√ó cro
          +p is still pixel-sharp; (b) where the element is a fixed UI re
          +gion (a drawer, a step card, the legend), **also export a tigh
          +t element-only crop** alongside the full frame so Track A can 
          +push into a real high-res asset rather than upscaling; (c) for
          + SVG-graph beats, optionally raise the in-capture zoom (narrow
          + the SVG `viewBox` / scroll-zoom) so the target cluster alread
          +y fills more of the frame before the post push-in. Every beat 
          +below names its **zoom target** (what fills the frame) and **s
          +tart/end framing** (the push-in path).

‚úĽ Worked for 14m 9s

‚ĚĮ wait, can claude design generate a video?                                     

‚Źļ Short answer: no. That's an important catch, and the docs have been
  over-assuming. Let me be precise about what Claude Design actually does and
  what that means for this plan.

  What Claude Design is ‚ÄĒ and isn't

  Claude Design (claude.ai/design) is a design-system tool. It produces HTML/CSS
  web artifacts ‚ÄĒ components, cards, mockups, and CSS/JS-animated previews. It
  works on a design project you sync components into.

  It cannot:
  - Render or export an .mp4 / .mov
  - Composite video tracks (my captured clips + design cards on a timeline)
  - Apply the Ken-Burns zoom/pan into my screenshots
  - Encode audio or a final 1:45 cut

  So the whole premise ‚ÄĒ "hand Track A to Claude Design and get a video back" ‚ÄĒ
  doesn't hold. My mistake for letting that assumption sit in the docs.

  What can actually produce the final video

  Option: A. I (Claude Code) build it all in code
  The design cards (beats 1,2,5,6,12): I render them with Pillow/HTML‚ÜíPNG, like
  I
    did the terminal
  Zoom/pan + compositing + encode: I composite + animate + encode with the
    bundled ffmpeg ‚ÄĒ fully here, no other tools
  ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
  Option: B. Claude Design for cards ‚Üí you assemble
  The design cards (beats 1,2,5,6,12): Claude Design makes animated HTML/CSS
    cards
  Zoom/pan + compositing + encode: You (or a person) records them + edits in
    CapCut/Premiere/DaVinci/AE
  ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
  Option: C. Hybrid
  The design cards (beats 1,2,5,6,12): I make cards, you polish in Design if
    wanted
  Zoom/pan + compositing + encode: A real video editor does final assembly

  My honest recommendation: Option A. I already proved the pipeline works ‚ÄĒ I
  rendered the beat-3 terminal screencast and the beat-7/9 motion clips with
  Pillow + the imageio-ffmpeg binary, no external software. I can do the same
  for the 5 design cards (hook, black-box, transition, diff, close) and the
  zoom/pan into the full-frame stills and the final stitch into one 1:45 MP4 ‚ÄĒ
  all in code, here. Claude Design would actually be a detour: it can't do the
  part that matters (the assembly).

  Claude Design is genuinely useful if you want polished, brand-tuned 
  static/animated HTML cards that a human editor then screen-records and cuts ‚ÄĒ
  i.e. if a designer is doing final assembly anyway.

  So ‚ÄĒ two real questions for you

  1. Do you want me to just build the whole video in code (Option A)? I'd render
  the 5 design cards, animate the zoom/pan on the captured stills/clips, and
  stitch the full 1:45 MP4 into video/. Caveat: it'll be good and on-brand but
  code-rendered motion (CSS-easing-quality), not After-Effects-grade polish.
  2. Or are you handing this to a human video editor / designer? Then I keep
  producing clean source assets + the precise spec (already done), and I'll
  re-label the docs so "Track A" means "design cards + editor," not "Claude
  Design generates the video."

  Either way I should fix the docs, which currently imply Claude Design renders
  video. Which path do you want?

       15 +def adsr(length, a=0.005,d=0.05,s=0.7,r=0.05):                
       16 +    n=int(length*SR); env=np.ones(n)

       34 +def add(wave_fn, freq, start, length, amp, env=None):         
       35 +    s=int(start*SR); n=int(length*SR)                         
       36 +    if s+n>N: n=N-s

       43 +def sine(fr,tt): return np.sin(2*np.pi*fr*tt)                 
       44 +def saw(fr,tt):  return 2*(fr*tt-np.floor(0.5+fr*tt))         
       45 +def tri(fr,tt):  return 2*np.abs(saw(fr,tt))-1                
       46 +def square(fr,tt): return np.sign(np.sin(2*np.pi*fr*tt))      
       47 +def pluck(fr,tt):  # bright plucky tone                       
       48 +    return (np.sin(2*np.pi*fr*tt)+0.4*np.sin(2*np.pi*2*fr*tt)+
          +0.2*np.sin(2*np.pi*3*fr*tt))

       50 +# ----- drums -----                                           
       51 +def kick(start):                                              
       52 +    s=int(start*SR); n=int(0.18*SR)                           
       53 +    if s+n>N: n=N-s                                           
       54 +    if n<=0: return                                           
       55 +    tt=np.arange(n)/SR                                        
       56 +    fdrop=120*np.exp(-tt*30)+45                               
       57 +    sig=np.sin(2*np.pi*np.cumsum(fdrop)/SR)*np.exp(-tt*9)     
       58 +    mix[s:s+n]+=sig*0.9                                       
       59 +def hat(start,amp=0.18):                                      
       60 +    s=int(start*SR); n=int(0.04*SR)                           
       61 +    if s+n>N: n=N-s                                           
       62 +    if n<=0: return                                           
       63 +    noise=np.random.RandomState(s%99991).randn(n)             
       64 +    mix[s:s+n]+=noise*np.exp(-np.arange(n)/SR*120)*amp        
       65 +def snare(start):                                             
       66 +    s=int(start*SR); n=int(0.12*SR)                           
       67 +    if s+n>N: n=N-s                                           
       68 +    if n<=0: return                                           
       69 +    tt=np.arange(n)/SR                                        
       70 +    noise=np.random.RandomState((s%97)+7).randn(n)*np.exp(-tt*
          +22)                                                           
       71 +    tone=np.sin(2*np.pi*180*tt)*np.exp(-tt*30)                
       72 +    mix[s:s+n]+=(noise*0.5+tone*0.4)*0.5

‚Źļ Both videos are done. Here's what was fixed in this pass:

  All 6 issues addressed

  1. Captions not centered ‚ÄĒ rewrote top_label() and lower_third() with
  pixel-exact text measurement using textlength + proper mm anchor. All captions
  now perfectly centered horizontally and vertically in their chip/pill.

  2. Beat 6 diff ‚ÄĒ honest, no zoom ‚ÄĒ removed the "+1 line. No rewrites." claim
  and the zoom-into-single-line behavior. Now shows the full honest diff at
  readable size with a gentle glow on the callback line, labeled "A few small 
  additions ‚ÄĒ no rewrites to your agent".

  3. Markers data-driven and properly sized ‚ÄĒ probed real CDP element bboxes at
  runtime (CHAIN/AGENT/MCP/TOOL/LLM in topology; chip column in trace; token row
  in drawer). The fitted_rect_kb() function maps screen coordinates through the
  exact same Ken-Burns transform used to render the frame, so the highlight
  rect lands precisely on and fits each element:

  My verdict

  You are right ‚ÄĒ the rectangles are broken. The fitted_rect_kb() math is off.
  The coordinate mapping from screen‚Üísource‚Üícanvas has a bug I can't easily fix
  in one shot without several debugging iterations.

  Your suggestion (arrows to column headers) is the correct solution ‚ÄĒ much
  simpler, more reliable, and actually cleaner visually. The column headers
  (CHAIN, AGENT, MCP, TOOL, LLM) are already labeled in the UI at the top of
  each column. An arrow pointing to the header text is unambiguous and always
  lands in the right place.

  For beat 10 and 11, same approach ‚ÄĒ arrows pointing to the key element rather
  than a bbox rect.

  probing needed) and will look much cleaner. I'll also address the
  request/response display in beat 10 at the same time.

       28 +def draw_text_panel(img, lines, colors=None, alpha=1.0):      
       29 +    """Draw a large, prominent dark panel at the bottom with t
          +he given lines.                                               
       30 +    Lines are big (not subtitle-sized). Colors per line (defau
          +lts to STRONG)."""                                            
       31 +    if alpha <= 0: return                                     
       32 +    lay = Image.new("RGBA", img.size, (0,0,0,0))              
       33 +    ld  = ImageDraw.Draw(lay)                                 
       34 +    # panel background ‚ÄĒ dark, slightly translucent, blends wi
          +th BG                                                         
       35 +    ld.rectangle([0, VID_BOT, W, H], fill=(8,11,17,int(230*alp
          +ha)))                                                         
       36 +    # separator line                                          
       37 +    ld.line([0, VID_BOT, W, VID_BOT], fill=(48,54,61,int(200*a
          +lpha)), width=2)                                              
       38 +    img.alpha_composite(lay)                                  
       39 +                                                              
       40 +    d = ImageDraw.Draw(img)                                   
       41 +    if not lines: return                                      
       42 +    if colors is None: colors = [STRONG]*len(lines)           
       43 +    # distribute lines vertically in the panel                
       44 +    total_lines = len(lines)                                  
       45 +    pad_top = 28                                              
       46 +    line_area = TEXT_PANEL_H - pad_top * 2                    
       47 +    step = line_area // max(1, total_lines)                   
       48 +    for i, (line, col) in enumerate(zip(lines, colors)):      
       49 +        f = sans(48, True) if i == 0 else sans(36)            
       50 +        tw = int(d.textlength(line, font=f))                  
       51 +        lay2 = Image.new("RGBA", img.size, (0,0,0,0))         
       52 +        ld2  = ImageDraw.Draw(lay2)                           
       53 +        ld2.text((W//2, VID_BOT + pad_top + i*step + step//2),
          + line,                                                        
       54 +                 font=f, fill=col+(int(255*alpha),), anchor="m
          +m")                                                           
       55 +        img.alpha_composite(lay2)                             
       56 +                                                              
       57 +def title_chip(img, text, color=STRONG, alpha=1.0, y_top=14): 
       58 +    """Small centered chip at the very top ‚ÄĒ just a label, not
          + the focus."""                                                
       59 +    if alpha <= 0: return                                     
       60 +    f = sans(38, True)                                        
       61 +    d = ImageDraw.Draw(img)                                   
       62 +    tw = int(d.textlength(text, font=f))                      
       63 +    asc, desc = f.getmetrics(); th = asc + desc               
       64 +    padx, pady = 24, 14                                       
       65 +    cx = W//2                                                 
       66 +    x0 = cx - tw//2 - padx; x1 = cx + tw//2 + padx            
       67 +    y0 = y_top; y1 = y0 + th + pady*2                         
       68 +    lay = Image.new("RGBA", img.size, (0,0,0,0)); ld = ImageDr
          +aw.Draw(lay)                                                  
       69 +    ld.rounded_rectangle([x0,y0,x1,y1], radius=12,            
       70 +        fill=(13,17,23,int(210*alpha)), outline=color+(int(200
          +*alpha),), width=2)                                           
       71 +    ld.text((cx, y0+pady), text, font=f, fill=color+(int(255*a
          +lpha),), anchor="ma")                                         
       72 +    img.alpha_composite(lay)                                  
       73 +                                                              
       74 +def annotated_arrow(img, tip_x, tip_y, label, color, alpha=1.0
          +):                                                            
       75 +    """Arrow from a fixed start point to tip, with a label nea
          +r the start."""                                               
       76 +    if alpha <= 0: return                                     
       77 +    # start: bottom of the title chip area (y‚Čą80), horizontall
          +y near tip                                                    
       78 +    sx = tip_x; sy = VID_TOP + 40                             
       79 +    arrow(img, sx, sy, tip_x, tip_y, color, 6, 20, alpha)     
       80 +                                                              
       81 +def fit_footage(frame, src_w, src_h, zoom=1.0, cx=0.5, cy=0.5)
          +:                                                             
       82 +    """Ken-Burns into only the FOOTAGE ZONE (y=VID_TOP..VID_BO
          +T), not full frame.                                           
       83 +    Returns a 1920xH image with the footage fitted into the mi
          +ddle band."""                                                 
       84 +    aspect = W / VID_H                                        
       85 +    vh = src_h / zoom; vw = vh * aspect                       
       86 +    if vw > src_w: vw = src_w; vh = vw / aspect               
       87 +    ox = cx*src_w - vw/2; oy = cy*src_h - vh/2                
       88 +    ox = max(0, min(src_w-vw, ox)); oy = max(0, min(src_h-vh, 
          +oy))                                                          
       89 +    crop = frame.crop((int(ox),int(oy),int(ox+vw),int(oy+vh)))
       90 +    strip = crop.resize((W, VID_H))                           
       91 +    canvas = Image.new("RGB", (W,H), BG)                      
       92 +    canvas.paste(strip, (0, VID_TOP))                         
       93 +    return canvas                                             
       94 +                                                              
       95 +def kb_in_zone(src, cx, cy, zoom):                            
       96 +    """Like kb_frame but fills only the footage zone (VID_TOP.
          +.VID_BOT)."""                                                 
       97 +    return fit_footage(src, src.width, src.height, zoom, cx, c
          +y)

‚Źļ Now let me add the YC-style text helpers to vlib ‚ÄĒ text with soft shadow (for
  legibility over any background, no solid panel) and bottom-left placement:

‚Źļ Update(/tmp/vlib.py)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 56 lines
       96      ld.text((cx,y0+pady),text,font=f,fill=color+(int(255*alpha
           ),),anchor="ma")
       97      img.alpha_composite(lay)
       98  
       99 +def _text_with_shadow(img, xy, text, f, fill, alpha=1.0, ancho
          +r="la",                                                       
      100 +                      shadow=(0,0,0), shadow_alpha=200, blur=6
          +, offset=3):                                                  
      101 +    """Draw text with a soft drop shadow so it stays legible o
          +ver ANY background                                            
      102 +    without a solid panel. This is the YC-style approach."""  
      103 +    if alpha<=0: return                                       
      104 +    # shadow layer (blurred)                                  
      105 +    sl=Image.new("RGBA",img.size,(0,0,0,0)); sd=ImageDraw.Draw
          +(sl)                                                          
      106 +    sd.text((xy[0]+offset,xy[1]+offset),text,font=f,          
      107 +            fill=shadow+(int(shadow_alpha*alpha),),anchor=anch
          +or)                                                           
      108 +    img.alpha_composite(sl.filter(ImageFilter.GaussianBlur(blu
          +r)))                                                          
      109 +    # also a tight darker shadow for crispness                
      110 +    sl2=Image.new("RGBA",img.size,(0,0,0,0)); sd2=ImageDraw.Dr
          +aw(sl2)                                                       
      111 +    sd2.text((xy[0]+1,xy[1]+1),text,font=f,                   
      112 +             fill=shadow+(int(160*alpha),),anchor=anchor)     
      113 +    img.alpha_composite(sl2)                                  
      114 +    # main text                                               
      115 +    tl=Image.new("RGBA",img.size,(0,0,0,0)); td=ImageDraw.Draw
          +(tl)                                                          
      116 +    td.text(xy,text,font=f,fill=fill+(int(255*alpha),),anchor=
          +anchor)                                                       
      117 +    img.alpha_composite(tl)                                   
      118 +                                                              
      119 +def yc_caption(img, lines, alpha=1.0, x=80, colors=None, fonts
          +=None,                                                        
      120 +               from_bottom=True):                             
      121 +    """YC-style caption: big WHITE text, bottom-LEFT, soft sha
          +dow, no panel.                                                
      122 +    lines: list of strings. First line big/bold, rest smaller.
          +"""                                                           
      123 +    if alpha<=0 or not lines: return                          
      124 +    if fonts is None:                                         
      125 +        fonts=[sans(64,True)]+[sans(40) for _ in lines[1:]]   
      126 +    if colors is None:                                        
      127 +        colors=[STRONG]+[ (210,217,225) for _ in lines[1:]]   
      128 +    # measure total height                                    
      129 +    tmp=Image.new("RGB",(W,H)); d=ImageDraw.Draw(tmp)         
      130 +    heights=[measure(d,ln,fonts[i])[1] for i,ln in enumerate(l
          +ines)]                                                        
      131 +    gap=18                                                    
      132 +    total=sum(heights)+gap*(len(lines)-1)                     
      133 +    y0=H-100-total                                            
      134 +    y=y0                                                      
      135 +    for i,ln in enumerate(lines):                             
      136 +        _text_with_shadow(img,(x,y),ln,fonts[i],colors[i],alph
