  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Done. New minimal example created at examples/mcp/support_demo/.

  What it is ‚ÄĒ Customer Support Assistant

  The simplest possible "agent over MCP" story, deliberately tiny so it reads
  clearly on video:

  ‚Ėé A customer asks "Where is my order A1043, and what's your delivery policy?" 
  ‚Ėé ‚Üí the agent looks up the order, checks shipping + the policy, and drafts a 
  ‚Ėé reply.

  2 servers √ó 2 tools = 5 clean nodes (vs. the trip demo's 22):
  - orders server ‚Üí look_up_order, get_shipping_status
  - kb server ‚Üí search_help_center, get_policy
  - Local ‚Üí draft_reply

  Files (mirrors trip_demo exactly)

  - before.py ‚ÄĒ same agent, no tracesage (noisy hand-rolled DEBUG logs ‚Üí final
  reply)
  - demo.py ‚ÄĒ same agent + the minimal tracesage wiring
  - orders_server.py + kb_server.py ‚ÄĒ bundled stdio MCP servers, hardcoded
  realistic data
  - README.md

  Verified

  - ‚úÖ Compiles, ruff clean
  - ‚úÖ demo.py runs end-to-end ‚Üí agent calls all 4 tools correctly, drafts an
  accurate reply, tracesage attributes orders (2) ¬∑ kb (2) ¬∑ Local (1)
  - ‚úÖ before.py produces the intended "before" pain (log wall + final answer)
  - ‚úÖ Uses the same .env / LLM_PROVIDER setup as the trip demo (your OpenAI key
  works)
  - ‚úÖ Listed in examples/mcp/README.md as the recommended simplest starting
  point

  Why it's good for the YC video

  It conveys the whole value prop in one glance: "an AI agent doing real 
  business work (support), and tracesage shows you exactly which tool from which
  server it called, what each returned, and what it cost." The 5-node topology
  is uncluttered, the customer-support scenario is instantly relatable to any
  viewer, and the before/after contrast is stark ‚ÄĒ the silent log-wall vs. the
  clean traced UI.

  Want me to record screenshots/footage of this demo for an alternate (cleaner)
  cut of the launch video, or is the example itself enough for now?

‚Źļ The cleanest demo: two runs under one tracer ‚ÄĒ run 1 succeeds (order A1043,
  shipped), run 2 fails (order A1044, whose shipping lookup hits a downstream
  error). The trace will show a red error node pinpointing exactly which tool

  server able to fail realistically:

‚Źļ "Sorry, need more steps to process this request" = recursion limit hit. The
  forced tool_choice="look_up_order" makes the model call that tool on every
  turn ‚ÄĒ the MCP tool error doesn't propagate as a Python exception (MCP returns
  an error result, the adapter feeds it back to the model), so it loops forever
  until recursion limit, then exits gracefully (no raise ‚Üí run marked
  completed).

  Let me test in isolation what the MCP tool error actually does:

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Root cause confirmed: FastMCP converts a tool's exception into an error result
  across the stdio boundary ‚ÄĒ langchain-mcp-adapters returns it as tool content
  ("Error executing tool look_up_order: ... shard 'orders-02' unavailable 
  ..."), NOT a Python raise. So ToolNode(handle_tool_errors=False) never sees an
  exception, and with forced tool_choice the agent loops to the recursion limit
  and exits gracefully ‚Üí run marked completed.

  Let me check if langchain-mcp-adapters can be told to raise on MCP errors:

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ The installed adapter DOES support raising on MCP errors. Lines 272-273 raise
  _MCPToolExecutionError when isError=True, governed by a handle_tool_errors
  param on the conversion functions. Let me check those signatures:

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ The adapter supports handle_tool_errors=False, which makes the MCP tool raise
  _MCPToolExecutionError (a ToolException) instead of returning error content.
  But MultiServerMCPClient.get_tools() and our register_mcp_client use the
  default True. Let me check how register_mcp_client loads tools:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ The fix: the MCP tool must raise instead of returning error content.
  langchain-mcp-adapters supports this via handle_tool_errors=False at load
  time. Let me verify that the loaded tool then raises, and whether
  MultiServerMCPClient.get_tools accepts the kwarg:

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)
‚Źļ load_mcp_tools(session, handle_tool_errors=False) makes the MCP tool raise ‚ÄĒ
  but get_tools() (the fast path in register_mcp_client) doesn't accept that
  kwarg. The clean fix is to add an optional handle_tool_errors to
  register_mcp_client so callers can choose whether MCP tool errors fail the run
  (and route through the session loader when they want raising). This is a
  genuinely useful library knob and makes the demo's failure path work. Let me
  add it:

‚Źļ Now BOTH fail with ClosedResourceError ‚ÄĒ the session loader binds tools to a
  session that closes when the async with exits. The default get_tools() path
  creates fresh per-call sessions via connection. I need to pass connection (not
  an open session) to load_mcp_tools so each call opens its own session. Let me
  check the client's connection config:

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

  passing connection instead of an open session makes each tool call open its
  own fresh session (the pattern get_tools uses). Let me rewrite
  _load_server_tools to use connection:

‚Źļ Update(src/tracesage/adapters/mcp.py)

      59      )
      60  
      61  
      62 +def _server_connection(client: Any, server: str) -> Any | None:
      63 +    """Best-effort extraction of one server's connection config
         + from the client."""                                           
      64 +    for attr in ("connections", "_connections", "server_name_to
         +_config"):                                                     
      65 +        conns = getattr(client, attr, None)                    
      66 +        if isinstance(conns, dict) and server in conns:        
      67 +            return conns[server]                               
      68 +    return None                                                
      69 +                                                               
      70 +                                                               
      71  async def _load_server_tools(
      72      client: Any, server: str, *, handle_tool_errors: bool = Tru
          e
      73  ) -> list[Any]:

‚Źļ One test fails because my new code prefers the connection path before falling
  back to session(). Let me look at the test to understand its intent:

  Read 1 file, ran 2 shell commands (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ The test test_load_server_tools_session_fallback models an old adapter where
  get_tools(server_name=...) fails, expecting a fall-through to session(). My
  change inserts a preferred connection-based path before the session
  last-resort. The test's _fake_load only accepts session. Since the connection
  path is now the correct primary fallback (it's how tool calls get a fresh
  session), I'll update the test to verify both: connection path preferred,
  session as genuine last resort.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚Źļ Done. The support_demo now demonstrates exactly what you asked for ‚ÄĒ multiple 
  runs, one succeeding and one failing ‚ÄĒ which is the library's core value
  proposition.

  What was broken and what I fixed

  Root cause: The A1044 "failure" run kept showing as completed. Two layers were
  hiding the failure:
  1. look_up_order had A1044 in the _ORDERS dict, so it returned data before
  reaching the raise (unreachable code).
  2. Even after fixing that, FastMCP converts a tool's exception into an error
  result across the stdio boundary ‚ÄĒ langchain-mcp-adapters then returns it as
  tool content, not a Python raise. So ToolNode(handle_tool_errors=False) never
  saw an exception; with the forced tool_choice it just looped to the recursion
  limit and exited gracefully.

  Fixes:
  - orders_server.py ‚ÄĒ moved the A1044 raise before the dict lookup and removed
  A1044 from _ORDERS, so the down-shard error always fires.
  - src/tracesage/adapters/mcp.py ‚ÄĒ added a handle_tool_errors parameter to
  register_mcp_client() (and the internal loader). When False, MCP tools are
  loaded so a server-side error raises and fails the run. Loading now binds
  tools to the server's connection config (not a transient session), so each
  call opens a fresh session ‚ÄĒ fixing a ClosedResourceError that surfaced along
  the way. This is a genuine, reusable library knob mirroring
  ToolNode(handle_tool_errors=...).
  - after.py / before.py ‚ÄĒ both now run the two tickets with identical agent
  construction (dropped the tool_choice machinery); the only difference between
  the files is the tracesage wiring.
  - README ‚ÄĒ rewritten around the success+failure story.
  - Tests ‚ÄĒ split the old session-fallback test into connection-fallback +
  session-last-resort, and added an end-to-end test asserting
  handle_tool_errors=False raises on A1044 (and still returns A1043).

  Verification: A1043 ‚Üí completed (67 steps, draft reply); A1044 ‚Üí failed (19
  steps) with a tool_error node on look_up_order carrying the exact order_id +
  shard error. Attribution intact (orders 2 ¬∑ kb 2 ¬∑ Local 1). Full suite green:
  205 passed, ruff clean.
‚úĽ Cogitated for 1h 52m 44s

     ‚Ēā pattern from beats_design.py), and a live click‚Üíview-switch clip for    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā topology‚Üítrace.                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Audio: reuse the upbeat 112-BPM bed (audio.py); regenerate to the     ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā final length.                                                           ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā ---                                                                     ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā 4. Beat-by-beat frame plan (~1:50, 11 beats)                            ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Each beat: Visual ¬∑ Transition in ¬∑ Text (content + position +          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā font/size) ¬∑ Motion.                                                    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Durations sum to ~110s; crossfades subtract ~3s ‚Üí ~1:47 final.          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Beat 1 ‚ÄĒ Hook (0:00‚Äď0:09) ¬∑ design card                                 ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Visual: near-black #0B0E14, drifting ghost-graph behind (reuse        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā ghost() in                                                              ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā beats_design.py). Subtle logo badge fades in bottom-center, dim.        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Transition in: from black.                                            ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Text: line 1 Your AI agent just ran. (Inter 40, dim, center ~40% H) ‚Üí ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā hold ‚Üí line 2                                                           ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Do you actually know what it did? (Inter 92 Bold, white, center ~52%    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā H), one soft                                                            ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā blue glow pulse on the question.                                        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Motion: staggered fade+rise; ghost graph drifts 0.5px/frame.          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Beat 2 ‚ÄĒ The real scenario (0:09‚Äď0:18) ¬∑ design animation               ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Visual: one Agent hexagon (green #2ea043) center; arrows fan out to   ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā two MCP                                                                 ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā server cards ‚ÄĒ orders and kb (cyan family) ‚ÄĒ each with its tool chips   ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā (look_up_order, get_shipping_status; search_help_center, get_policy) +  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā a local                                                                 ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā draft_reply chip. Nodes pop in sequence; arrows draw-on.                ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Transition in: crossfade from hook.                                   ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Text: bottom-left One request. (Inter 68 Bold) / The agent picks the  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā tools, the order, the calls. (Inter 38, dim).                           ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Motion: agent scale-pop, then each server+tools group fades in        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā left‚Üíright.                                                             ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Beat 3 ‚ÄĒ Flying blind (0:18‚Äď0:27) ¬∑ design animation (continues Beat 2) ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Visual: the same graph; edges + nodes desaturate and blur into "fog"  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā (Gaussian ramp),                                                        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā question-mark haze settling over the connections.                       ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Transition in: continuous from Beat 2 (no cut ‚ÄĒ the graph we just     ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā built dissolves).                                                       ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Text: bottom-left Every run can take a different path. (Inter¬†68) ‚Üí   ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā second line fades                                                       ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā And you can't see any of it. (Inter 38, dim).                           ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Motion: progressive blur 0‚Üí6px, opacity 1‚Üí0.35 over ~3s.              ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Beat 4 ‚ÄĒ The pain, shown (0:27‚Äď0:41) ¬∑ colored terminal footage ‚Üź       ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā requirement #1                                                          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Visual: a Pillow-rendered scrolling terminal (not a screenshot) of    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā the real                                                                ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā before.py log wall, ANSI-colored so the problem is unmistakable:        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   - dim grey #6e7681 for DEBUG httpcore/openai/MCP noise,               ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   - yellow #d29922 for WARNING lines,                                   ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   - red #f85149 for ERROR lines and the A1044 _MCPToolExecutionError    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā traceback (orders DB error: shard 'orders-02' unavailable‚Ä¶),            ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   - normal #c9d1d9 for the drafted A1043 reply.                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   - Source text is captured live by running before.py (we already have  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā the output); a                                                          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā small builder script colorizes by log level + traceback regex.          ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Transition in: crossfade from the fog.                                ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Text: bottom-left, over a soft scrim, When something's off, (Inter 68 ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Bold) / this is what you're working with. (Inter¬†38). At ~0:38 a red    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā underline/pulse on the                                                  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā shard 'orders-02' unavailable ERROR line.                               ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Motion: logs auto-scroll fast, then settle and hold on the red        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā traceback (the silence                                                  ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā on the error is the beat).                                              ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Beat 5 ‚ÄĒ The questions (0:41‚Äď0:57) ¬∑ design over dimmed logs ‚Üź core     ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā - Visual: freeze the terminal, dim to ~34% + dark vignette              ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā (Image.blend).                                                          ‚Ēā

     ‚Ēā - Text: four questions fade in one at a time, center, each Inter 60     ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā Bold; last                                                              ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā one brightest, earlier ones step to #9AA4AE. End with all four held:    ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   a. Did it call everything it should have?                             ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   b. Did it pick well, or grab the first option?                        ‚Ēā
     ‚Ēā   c. How much did the run cost?                                         ‚Ēā

     ‚Ēā the thesis)                                                             ‚Ēā

     ‚Ēā                                                                         ‚Ēā

  ‚éŅ ¬†Plan saved to: ~/.claude/plans/snazzy-chasing-dongarra.md ¬∑ /plan to edit
     video-new ‚ÄĒ tracesage YC demo (silent, ~1:58, support_demo)

     Context

     A new silent, visual-first launch video for tracesage, built in a fresh
     video-new/
     folder, featuring the new support_demo example (orders + kb MCP servers)
     instead
     of the old trip_demo. It must capture attention through
     streaming/transition motion and
     on-screen type that reads like a real launch video (Linear/Vercel/Raycast
     style), not
     subtitles.

     Two things shape this beyond the original 10-beat storyboard:

     1. The failure run is the payoff. support_demo produces TWO runs under one
     tracer
     ‚ÄĒ A1043 completes (green) and A1044 fails (look_up_order ‚Üí
     orders DB error: shard 'orders-02' unavailable). The UI natively renders
     the
     failed run in red: red status badge (.status-badge.failed ‚Üí --error 
     #f85149), a
     red-stroked error node on the look_up_order tool node (.node-group.error 
     .node-shape), and a red timeline error card. This is the literal answer to
     the
     storyboard's "If it's wrong, where do you start?" ‚ÄĒ the back half builds to
     it.
     2. The pain beat shows colored logs ‚ÄĒ red ERROR, yellow WARNING, dim DEBUG,
     and the
     red A1044 traceback ‚ÄĒ so the viewer feels the problem (user requirement
     #1).

     We reuse the proven code-based pipeline (Pillow + bundled ffmpeg via
     imageio_ffmpeg,
     no editor), copied into video-new/ and adapted. Everything renders
     deterministically.

     Verified DB facts (~/.tracesage/support-demo/traces.db):
     - 019f0ced-b82c-7990-be05-fbc14457ae82 ‚ÄĒ completed, 67 steps (A1043).
     - 019f0ced-e157-7621-b906-54dd5becd3bb ‚ÄĒ failed, 19 steps (A1044). Its
     journey
     (queried by root_run_id, as the UI builds it) DOES include tool_start ‚Üí
     tool_error on look_up_order (child run 019f0ced-e534‚Ä¶), then chain_error
     up to LangGraph. ‚áí Beat 10 can show a red error node on the look_up_order 
     tool
     node. (A naive run_id-only query misses this and shows only chain_error;
     always
     query by root_run_id.)
     - ~38 orphan running/0-step LangGraph sub-runs exist; the run list must
     show only the
     2 root runs ‚ÄĒ verify before capture, filter in JS if needed (¬ß7).

     ---
     1. Folder setup ‚ÄĒ video-new/

     Create video-new/ and copy the pipeline from video/ as the starting point
     (do NOT
     mutate video/, which documents the shipped trip_demo cut): vlib.py,
     beats_footage.py, beats_design.py, stitch2.py, audio.py, cap.py. Copy
     assets/tracesage-logo-badge.png into video-new/assets/. New dirs:
     video-new/assets/,
     video-new/beats/, video-new/fonts/. New file video-new/STORYBOARD.md (human
     beat
     sheet = ¬ß4).

     Path-repointing + bug fixes in the copies (REQUIRED ‚ÄĒ current code has live
     bugs)

     - vlib.py:8 OUT=".../video" ‚Üí absolute path of video-new. (stitch2.py
     B/audio
     and cap.py follow from V.OUT.)
     - vlib.py fonts ‚Üí bundle paths with safe fallback (see ¬ß2). Fix the 
     sans(bold=...)
     no-op: today sans() ignores bold (the SF TTC has no separate bold face),
     which is
     why headlines never render bold. Route bold=True to Inter-Bold.ttf.
     - beats_footage.py:5 and beats_design.py:5 sys.path.insert(0,"/tmp") ‚Üí
     sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).parent)).
     - beats_design.py:10 stale import will ImportError on run ‚ÄĒ it imports
     draw_text_panel, VID_TOP, VID_BOT, TEXT_PANEL_H, fit_footage, kb_in_zone
     which no
     longer exist in beats_footage.py. Keep only title_chip.
     - cap.py:7 OUT ‚Üí video-new/assets. audio.py:11 OUT ‚Üí
     video-new/audio_bed.wav;
     set DUR after measuring final length (¬ß6).
     - stitch2.py order list ‚Üí the new 11-beat ordering (¬ß4), beats renamed
     beat1..beat11.

     ---
     2. Typography ‚ÄĒ final spec (YC launch style; user requirement #2)

     Fonts bundled into video-new/fonts/ via fonts_setup.py (both already on
     disk, no
     download): Inter Inter-Bold.ttf + Inter-Regular.ttf (from
     ~/Downloads/truefoundry-seo-blog-writer/assets/fonts/); JetBrains Mono
     JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf + -Bold.ttf (from
     /Applications/PyCharm.app/Contents/jbr/Contents/Home/lib/fonts/). Safe
     fallback in
     vlib.py: if a bundle font is missing, fall back to SFNS.ttf / Menlo.ttc so
     renders
     never crash.

     Why it reads as designed text, not subtitles: headline 72‚Äď88px is ~2√ó
     subtitle norm
     (32‚Äď38px); bottom-LEFT with open whitespace above (no caption band); soft
     shadow via
     _text_with_shadow (blur 6‚Äď10) instead of a panel; ‚Č§2 lines / beat, L1 <7
     words, L2 <12
     words. The question beat (5) is deliberately center-staged as a "statement
     slide."

     Terminal ANSI colors (Beat 4): DEBUG #6e7681 ¬∑ INFO/NORMAL #c9d1d9 ¬∑
     WARNING
     #d29922 ¬∑ ERROR/TRACEBACK #f85149 (bold 28px) ¬∑ A1043 draft reply #3fb950.

     ---
     3. Final copy ‚ÄĒ verbatim on-screen strings

     - B1 Hook ‚ÄĒ intro Your AI agent just did something. / hero Do you know what
     it actually did?
     - B2 Scenario ‚ÄĒ L1 One request. The agent picks everything. / L2 orders DB 
     ¬∑ knowledge base ¬∑ draft reply ‚ÄĒ all its call.
     - B3 Flying blind ‚ÄĒ L1 Every run can take a different path. / L2 You can't 
     see any of it.
     - B4 Pain ‚ÄĒ L1 When something's off ‚ÄĒ / L2 this is what you're debugging 
     with.
     (red glow on the shard-error line; the line itself is the copy)
     - B5 Questions (stagger; Q4 stays white):
       a. Did it call every tool it should have?
       b. Did it pick smart, or just grab the first?
       c. How much did this run actually cost?
       d. If it's wrong ‚ÄĒ where do you even start?
     - B6 Pivot ‚ÄĒ hero What if you could just look? (warm the word look)
     - B7 Reveal ‚ÄĒ chip tracesage ‚ÄĒ local, live (green) / L1 Now you can see 
     inside. /
     L2 Drops into what your team already built. / pill ‚óŹ localhost:7842 ‚ÄĒ live
     - B8 See & rewind ‚ÄĒ topo chip Topology ‚ÄĒ your whole system (blue); L1 Every
     agent, server, and tool. / L2 This is your architecture at runtime. ‚ÄĒ
     replay chip
     Run Trace ‚ÄĒ step by step (green); L1 Every step the agent took. / L2 Replay
     the whole run, start to finish. (TOPO_STOPS arrow heads AGENT/MCP
     SERVERS/LLM unchanged)
     - B9 Cost & proof ‚ÄĒ chip Click any step ‚ÄĒ full detail (blue); L1 What it 
     chose. What it cost. / L2-A Token counts, latency, the full request 
     payload. / L2-B
     816 in ¬∑ 83 out ¬∑ 899 total ‚ÄĒ per call. (USE REAL A1043 numbers ‚ÄĒ query DB,
     see ¬ß5) /
     pill ‚úď proves the tools ran, not just claimed to (green)
     - B10 When it breaks ‚ÄĒ chip A1044 ‚ÄĒ failed run (red); L1 When it breaks, 
     you see exactly where. / L2 One red node. The tool, the input, the error. /
     callback pill
     ‚Ü≥ "where do you even start?" ‚ÄĒ right here. (green; verbatim echo of B5 Q4,
     keep "even")
     - B11 Close ‚ÄĒ tagline See what your AI is actually doing. / open source ¬∑ 
     free ¬∑ MIT / chip pip install "tracesage[mcp]" /
     github.com/kjgpta/tracesage

     Callback chains (audit before render): "actually" (B1/B9/B11); "where do
     you even
     start?" (B5 Q4 ‚ÜĒ B10 pill, verbatim); "see inside" (B6‚ÜíB7). In a silent
     video, repeated
     vocabulary is how the viewer feels narrative progression.

     (stitch2.py). Text entrance = alpha=ease((t-delay)/0.6), rise 14px.

     5. New files & Beat-4 terminal spec

     - fonts_setup.py ‚ÄĒ copies Inter + JBMono into video-new/fonts/ (no
     network).
     - log_capture.py ‚ÄĒ runs before.py once (needs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY), tees real
     output to video-new/assets/before_log.txt (commit it ‚Üí reproducible).
     Exports
     load_log_lines(path).
     - terminal.py ‚ÄĒ Pillow colored-log renderer. Public:
     render_terminal_video(log_lines, *, duration, fps=30, w=1920, h=1080, 
     font_path, font_size=27, bg=(11,14,20), line_height_mult=1.45, 
     scroll_end_s=9.0) -> (frames, shard_line_bbox). Also saves
     assets/b04_terminal_hold.png (the hold frame)
     so Beat 5 loads it directly instead of depending on Beat 4's render order.
       - classify_line() ‚Üí DEBUG/INFO/WARNING/ERROR/TRACEBACK/NORMAL/BLANK via
     regex
     ^\d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}\s+(DEBUG|INFO|WARNING|ERROR|CRITICAL), plus
     Traceback/indented
     continuation ‚Üí TRACEBACK. Color map = the ANSI table in ¬ß2.
       - Scroll fast (‚Čą200 lines / 9s) so the shard line lands lower-third at
     scroll_end_s,
     then hold. Shadow only on ERROR/TRACEBACK lines (cheap for dim DEBUG).
     Returns the
     shard line's canvas bbox for the Beat-4 glow.
     - B9 real numbers: query SELECT token_input,token_output FROM events WHERE 
     root_run_id='019f0ced-b82c-‚Ä¶' AND event_type='llm_end' and use the first
     call's
     values in the L2-B caption (do NOT carry over trip_demo's 816/83/899 if
     they differ).

     pop-ins fill

     crash).

     server prints
     (or force --port 7842); add the orphan-row JS filter if needed.

     A1044 is the

     before
     shooting.

  sans(bold) no-op so bold headlines actually render bold:

‚Źļ Update(video-new/vlib.py)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 14 lines, removed 4 lines
      25  NODE_AGENT = (46,160,67)
      26  FLIGHTS=(230,25,75); HOTELS=(240,50,230); WEATHER=(154,99,36)
      27  
      28 -SANS = "/System/Library/Fonts/SFNS.ttf"                        
      29 -MONO = "/System/Library/Fonts/Menlo.ttc"                       
      28 +# Bundled fonts (copied by fonts_setup.py). Fall back to system
         + faces so a render                                             
      29 +# never crashes if the bundle is missing.                      
      30 +_FONTS = pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "fonts"     
      31 +def _pick(name, fallback):                                     
      32 +    p = _FONTS / name                                          
      33 +    return str(p) if p.exists() else fallback                  
      34 +SANS      = _pick("Inter-Regular.ttf",      "/System/Library/Fo
         +nts/SFNS.ttf")                                                 
      35 +SANS_BOLD = _pick("Inter-Bold.ttf",         "/System/Library/Fo
         +nts/SFNS.ttf")                                                 
      36 +MONO      = _pick("JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf", "/System/Library

      198 +def ltor_wipe(img_old, img_new, progress):                    
      199 +    """Reveal img_new from the left edge over img_old. progres
          +s 0‚Üí1."""                                                     
      200 +    x=int(W*ease(progress))                                   
      201 +    out=img_old.copy()                                        
      202 +    if x>0: out.paste(img_new.crop((0,0,x,H)),(0,0))          
      203 +    return out                                                
      204 +                                                              
      205 +def _pivot_card(warm):

          +"""

          +3)

          +)

      216 +    f=sans(88,True)                                           
      217 +    words=["What","if","you","could","just","look?"]

      219 +    wsz=[measure(d,wd,f)[0] for wd in words]                  
      220 +    total=sum(wsz)+spw*(len(words)-1)                         
      221 +    x=W//2-total//2; yb=int(H*0.52)

          +="la",blur=8)

       67 +def label_with_arrow(img, lines, color, anchor_xy, target_xy, 
          +alpha=1.0):

          + clean arrow to a node."""

          +pha)

       80 +    block_cx=ax+block_w//2

‚Źļ Bug in Beat 10 Phase B: the red circle landed on LangGraph (left) instead of
  the look_up_order red node ‚ÄĒ my inverse coordinate mapping was inconsistent
  with full_frame's crop. Let me fix it with a single helper that uses the exact
  same formula as full_frame, so the circle always lands on the node.

‚Źļ Update(video-new/beats_footage.py)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 15 lines, removed 8 lines
      284              alpha=a, scrim=True, fonts=[sans(64,True),sans(38)
           ],
      285              colors=[STRONG,(210,217,225)])
      286          w.add(img.convert("RGB"))
      287 -    # Phase B (9s): push into the red look_up_order node (canv
          -as 388,508 at z1.0)                                           
      287 +    # Phase B (9s): push into the red look_up_order node.     
      288 +    # Node source-fraction (measured): (0.202, 0.470). _canvas
          +_of uses the SAME                                             
      289 +    # crop math as full_frame so the glow always lands on the 
          +node.                                                         
      290      segB=int(9*FPS)
      291 +    NFX,NFY=0.202,0.470                                       
      292 +    def _canvas_of(fx,fy,z,cx,cy,sw,sh):                      
      293 +        aspect=W/H; vh=sh/z; vw=vh*aspect                     
      294 +        if vw>sw: vw=sw; vh=vw/aspect                         
      295 +        ox=cx*sw-vw/2; oy=cy*sh-vh/2                          
      296 +        ox=max(0,min(sw-vw,ox)); oy=max(0,min(sh-vh,oy))

‚Źļ Good observation, and I dug into the actual code ‚ÄĒ what you're seeing isn't
  quite what it looks like, so let me explain precisely, then answer the "is
  there a provision" question.

  What's actually happening

  There are two separate reds in play, and they have different jobs:

  1. Node fill = the MCP server's identity color, from a hardcoded palette in
  graph.js:
  const MCP_PALETTE = ['#e6194B','#f58231','#f032e6','#ff5cb5','#9A6324','#80000
  0','#ff7f50','#c2255c'];
  1. The comment says it deliberately avoids the kind colors (agent green, llm
  blue, etc.) ‚ÄĒ but it's "warm reds/oranges/pinks/magentas only." And the hash
  lands your two servers on red-family fills: orders ‚Üí #800000 (dark red), kb ‚Üí 
  #c2255c (crimson). So healthy orders tools are filled dark-red in every run.
  That's the misleading part you spotted.
  2. Node outline (stroke) = the actual pass/fail signal: green ring if the node
  ran OK, red ring (--error #f85149) only if it errored (graph.js:1057 +
  .node-group.error CSS, styles.css:707). So the failed node is already
  distinguished ‚ÄĒ by a red outline ‚ÄĒ it's just drowned out because orders is
  already red-filled.

  So the failure logic is fine; the collision is that the server palette 
  includes the error-red family, which makes a red ring on an already-red node
  nearly invisible, and makes healthy red-filled tools look alarming.

  Is there a provision (config) for it?

  No. I checked config.py, the Python API, the server, and CSS ‚ÄĒ MCP_PALETTE is
  hardcoded in graph.js. The only color knobs exposed are the CSS variables
  (--node-agent/tool/llm/..., --error, --success), and the palette isn't one of
  them. So changing this is a small code edit, not a setting.

  The constraint is real: per-server colors currently must dodge agent-green,
  tool-amber, llm-blue, retriever-purple, chain-gray, mcp-teal ‚ÄĒ and now
  error-red too. Here's how I'd resolve it:

‚Źļ¬†User answered Claude's questions:
  ‚éŅ ¬†¬∑ How should I fix the red collision between MCP server fills and the error
     indicator? ‚Üí Recolor palette + bold error fill
     ¬∑ Where should this apply? ‚Üí Library only

  Summary

  Your instinct was right, and there was a genuine flaw worth fixing. Here's
  what I found and changed:

  The problem (two reds colliding):
  1. The MCP server palette in graph.js was "warm reds/oranges/pinks only," and
  the hash put your servers on red fills (orders ‚Üí dark red, kb ‚Üí crimson) ‚ÄĒ so
  healthy tools looked alarming in every run.
  2. The error signal was only a thin red outline, which was invisible on an
  already-red-filled node.

  Was there a config provision? No ‚ÄĒ I checked config.py, the Python API, the
  server, and CSS. MCP_PALETTE is hardcoded in graph.js; only the CSS theme vars
  are exposed. So this was a code fix, library-only, per your choice.

  What I changed (2 edits in src/tracesage/ui/graph.js):
  1. Recolored MCP_PALETTE to magenta/pink/violet/brown/orange ‚ÄĒ all clear of
  the kind colors (agent-green, tool-amber, llm-blue, retriever-purple,
  chain-gray, mcp-teal) and of error-red/success-green. Red is now reserved
  exclusively for failure.
  2. Bold red fill on error ‚ÄĒ _shapeForKind now fills a node solid --error red
  (not just a ring) when it has errors, overriding its kind/server color. The
  failure point is unmistakable.

  This propagates automatically to the legend and "Tools by source" panel (they
  import mcpServerColor). Tests all green, and the live UI confirms: completed
  runs have zero red, failed runs put solid red exactly on the node(s) that
  broke.

  Note: I left the video as-is per your "library only" choice ‚ÄĒ the existing
  video-new/tracesage_demo_zoom.mp4 still uses the old colors. Say the word if
  you later want me to re-capture the UI assets and re-render beats 8‚Äď10 so the
  demo reflects the new scheme.

      271 +    # row and never bleeds into the graph pane.               
      272 +    AZ,ACX,ACY=1.12,0.10,0.18                                 
      273 +    def _amap(fx,fy,sw,sh):                                   
      274 +        aspect=W/H; vh=sh/AZ; vw=vh*aspect                    
      275 +        if vw>sw: vw=sw; vh=vw/aspect

      277 +        ox=max(0,min(sw-vw,ox)); oy=max(0,min(sh-vh,oy))      
      278 +        return int((fx*sw-ox)/vw*W), int((fy*sh-oy)/vh*H)

          +side sidebar)

‚ĚĮ Based on stats recieved for the app, this is the summary, tell me what are    
  the next steps to be taken                                                    
                                                                                
                                                                                
  Your pre-launch baseline is now locked, and it's a clean zero. The last five  
  days ‚ÄĒ 06-24 through 06-28 ‚ÄĒ show zero `pip` installs, every single day. The  
  last pip install was 06-23, your 0.3.0 release day. Since you stopped cutting 
  releases, the genuine-install signal has been flat zero.                      
                                                                                
  That's the deduction, and it cuts two ways.                                   
                                                                                
  **The good news is measurement.** You now have the cleanest possible          
  experimental setup for next week. Baseline organic installs = 0. That means   
  after you post, the *first* non-release-day `pip` install is unambiguously a  
  real new user ‚ÄĒ no noise to subtract, no judgment call. Most people launch    
  without a clean baseline and can't tell signal from churn. You can. The first 
  nonzero pip day after launch is your win condition, full stop.                
                                                                                
  **The unwelcome news is what it confirms.** The social traffic that showed up 
  last turn ‚ÄĒ the t.co and Bluesky referrers, the visitor jump to 121, the      
  12th star ‚ÄĒ produced exactly zero installs. People are arriving, some are     
  sharing, a few are bookmarking, and not one has run the code. Your funnel     
  currently leaks 100% at the install step: ~121 visitors ‚Üí 12 stars ‚Üí 0        
  installs. That's the number that matters before a launch, because a launch    
  just multiplies the top of that funnel. More visitors times a zero install    
  rate is still zero.                                                           
                                                                                
  Everything currently pulling the package is automation, and the version       
  spread proves it: over the last five days, 0.1.0, 0.1.1, 0.2.0, and 0.2.1 are 
  *still* being downloaded ~10-12 times each, alongside 0.3.0. No human         
  installs four superseded versions of a library today ‚ÄĒ that's `bandersnatch`  
  (your largest recent bucket) mirroring every release to stay in sync, plus    
  browser/bot traffic. Not a single one of those recent pulls is `pip`.         
                                                                                
  One thing to not misread: this file starts 06-18, so its 57 total pip         
  installs isn't comparable to the 95 from the earlier file (which started      
  06-16 and caught that day's 38). Same data, narrower window ‚ÄĒ not a decline.  
                                                                                
  So going into next week: your success metric is now binary and clean, which   
  is the best position you've been in. But the data is telling you the launch   
  will likely convert eyeballs to stars and nothing further unless you fix the  
  install step first. The 0% install rate from people who are *already* finding 
  and sharing it isn't going to fix itself by adding more people. Spend this    
  week making the README convert a curious visitor into a `pip install` ‚ÄĒ the   
  runnable 30-second example and the topology diagram up top ‚ÄĒ then post.       
  Otherwise you'll get a bigger version of the exact pattern you have now: a    
  view spike, a few stars, and a pip line that stays at zero.

  108, below the fold and below the TOC. That's the lowest-friction "aha" you

  scroll past everything to find out.

  command" path. Verify it works end-to-end from a clean install, then feature

         +our logs.
